title: Robust Real-Time Brake Gap Estimation for EMB Systems Via Legendre-Based Two-Stage MFFRLS With Adaptive M-Estimator
authors: Teng Zuo, Huaicheng Yao, Yang Zhang, Wenpeng Wei
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: Brake gap is important in achieving precise force control and ensuring reliability of electromechanical brake (EMB) systems in autonomous vehicles. However, it is typically unmeasurable and time-varying due to progressive brake pad wear. This article proposes a Legendre-based multiple forgetting factors recursive least-squares (MFFRLS) with adaptive M-estimator algorithm for robust real-time brake gap estimation. Specifically, the estimation problem is first formulated into a regression problem inspired by the EMB data relationship. Then, Legendre basis is adopted for regressor transformation to improve estimation stability. After that, the MFFRLS with adaptive M-estimator is proposed to enhance robustness of brake gap estimation across the full life cycle. A two-stage estimation strategy is further designed to reduce computational burden. Estimation convergence analyses are established using Hessian matrix and Lyapunov theory. Comparison studies using green brake pad against standard recursive least squares, extended Kalman filter, Legendre-based RLS, and MFFRLS approaches substantiate the high estimation precision of the proposed algorithm, with steady-state error to be 0.51, 1.42, 0.54, 0.43, and 0.03 rad, respectively. Further validations show the root-mean-square error and relative RMSE of estimation are less than 0.20 rad and 2.99<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$e^{-4}$</tex-math></inline-formula>% across green, moderate and severely worn brake pads, demonstrating the adaptiveness and robustness of the proposed algorithm.

title: Magnetic sensor for real-time decoupling of tactile and non-tactile bimodal perception
authors: Yaru Wang, Ling Weng, Zhuolin Li, Huiwen Yang, Xiaokang Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Flexible sensors that detect multiple perceptual signals simultaneously are crucial for intelligent robots and human-machine interaction systems. Achieving deep fusion while enabling real-time decoupling of multiple sensing modalities has become a central challenge in this field. To address this, we designed a novel bimodal flexible sensor that simultaneously provides high-precision tactile and non-tactile sensing, combining high sensitivity with a relatively wide measurement range. The sensor consists of a mesh-structured magnetic thin film and integrates tunnel magneto resistance (TMR) sensing elements with a triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG) to form a multilayer flexible sensing system. The TMR elements detect magnetic-field variations induced either by applied force or by the approach of magnetic objects, thereby sensing pressure and distance. The TENG produces sharp transient voltage pulses exclusively at moments of contact or separation, supplying a reliable marker to discriminate between the two sensing modes. In tactile mode, experiments showed the sensor achieved a maximum sensitivity of 266.67 mV/N over a detection range of 0-18 N. The device demonstrated robust dynamic performance: after 1000 loading cycles, the output voltage did not exhibit significant degradation. This study realized real-time decoupling of tactile and non- tactile modalities by leveraging the transient signals generated by the triboelectric effect and provided a new solution to the signal-confusion problem in bimodal sensing.

title: NSDE-Net: An Observation-Driven Neural SDE Framework for State Estimation in Unknown Dynamical Systems
authors: Wei Yu, Yong Liu, Wenyi Wang, Yunxiang Jiang, Yunfei Zheng, Xiao Liu, Shiyuan Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: State estimation in unknown nonlinear dynamical systems has gained significant importance. However, existing methods often suffer from performance degradation when dealing with imperfect and incomplete observations, especially under nonlinear and non-Gaussian measurement conditions. To tackle this challenge, we propose a novel neural stochastic differential equation network (NSDE-Net) for efficient learning of system dynamics under non-ideal observations. The NSDE-Net models unknown system dynamics using a neural stochastic differential equation (SDE), where the hidden state is resolved through an observation-driven neural network. Specifically, the NSDE-Net employs an LSTM-based temporal modeling framework, enabling an observation encoder to create a direct mapping from observations to latent states. The drift and diffusion networks capture deterministic and stochastic dynamics, thereby reducing uncertainty in state estimation under non-ideal observations. This model is optimized through an observation likelihood function augmented with a Lipschitz prior regularization term. Additionally, we provide a theoretical analysis of the mean-square stability of the proposed NSDE-Net under suitable boundedness and Lipschitz conditions. Finally, simulations validate the stability of the proposed framework and the estimation performance of NSDE-Net under various observational conditions, while a real INS/GNSS navigation system is employed to evaluate the overall effectiveness of the algorithm.

title: $\mathcal {O}^{2}$-UCB: A Federated Multi-armed Bandit Mechanism in Zero Client-owned Data Network
authors: Hangfan Li, Yang Xu, Yibin Cai, Sheng Yue, Ziyu Peng, Yaoxue Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: The Federated Multi-Armed Bandit (FMAB) framework is proposed to facilitate collaborative model training in cloud-edge environments. Most existing FMAB-based systems assume that participants have personal datasets. This assumption becomes biased in scenarios with limited local data or when the discrepancy between historical data and online data cannot be quantified. Consequently, participants must passively collect data by offering services and gathering feedback from users within their charging areas. In addition, due to the mobility of the users, the number of requests is not stable. In such scenarios, effective model training requires addressing two key challenges. First, the allocation of resources for data collection at the edge is often mismatched with the actual number of service requests, resulting in limited training data and wasted resources. Second, in areas with sparse service requests, the lack of data further delays model adaptation. In this article, networks with these challenges are summarized as the training while collecting data federated bandit (TCF-bandit), and the over-area over-period upper confidence bound (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-UCB) algorithm is proposed to address two challenges. In the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-UCB algorithm, the cloud records request-generation patterns to mitigate resource waste caused by allocation mismatches resulting from unpredictable user mobility. Additionally, a weight-based global confidence radius is computed to assist areas with limited data in quickly identifying their optimal arms. Finally, we prove that the resource allocation waste and regret of the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-UCB algorithm exhibit sub-linear growth. We conduct experiments in different scenarios on the MovieLens, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 datasets to illustrate its superiority over SOTA methods by around 16.2%.

title: Safety and Performance Co-Optimization in Human–Robot Interaction: An Adaptive Energy Tank Design With Dynamic Control Barrier Functions
authors: Jiawen Yu, Jianing Liu, Weichao Sun
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: Theco-optimization of safety and task performance is a central challenge in physical human–robot interaction. Passivity ensures safety by limiting system energy, and impedance control enables compliant execution. However, conventional energy-tank methods often rely on fixed power constraints and produce discontinuous control signals, severely limiting adaptability in dynamic environments. To overcome these limitations, this article introduces a novel safety-aware passive control framework that unifies high-order dynamic control barrier functions (HD-CBFs) with an energy tank. Our contributions are twofold: an HD-CBF-based energy tank that continuously enforces time-varying power bounds, and an adaptive iterative learning scheme that autonomously optimizes power profiles for complex tasks without compromising safety. Extensive experiments on a 7-DOF Franka Emika Panda manipulator validate the framework’s robustness across key scenarios, including task interruption, unstructured environmental variations, and clinically relevant robotic ultrasound scanning. The system consistently maintains real-time power constraints and reduces force-tracking root-mean-square error by 53.9% compared to baselines. This demonstrates an effective balance between safety and performance, making it highly suitable for real-world human–robot collaboration.

title: Enhancing Coverage and Sensing in Space–Air–Ground Networks via a UAV-Assisted ISAC Framework with Satellite Backhaul
authors: Zhaoxia Ding, Zhaogui Zeng, Shuaishuai Guo, Ruidong Li, Ji Zhong, Tong-Xing Zheng, Hong-Ning Dai, Dengwang Li, Xuran Li
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Space–air–ground integrated networks (SAGINs) are expected to support ubiquitous communication and sensing in next-generation wireless systems. In this paper, we develop a stochastic-geometry-based analytical framework for a three-tier integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) network composed of ground base stations (BSs), unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), and low-earth-orbit (LEO) satellites. In the proposed architecture, ground BSs are spatially distributed according to an independent homogeneous Poisson point process (HPPP), UAVs perform sensing and uplink transmission with horizontal locations modeled by an HPPP, and LEO satellites provide relay/backhaul support with ground projections following an independent HPPP. Analytical expressions for the hop-wise communication coverage probability and the single-UAV sensing detection probability are derived. The end-to-end coverage probability is rigorously formulated as a joint event, and a tractable product-form expression is obtained by exploiting the independence of the three-tier spatial processes. Monte Carlo simulations validate the analysis and illustrate how satellite relaying, UAV deployment parameters, and propagation conditions affect communication reliability and sensing detectability. The proposed framework provides useful benchmark-level insights for the design of SAGIN-enabled ISAC systems and establishes analytical building blocks for future studies on cooperative sensing fusion.

title: Hybrid Domain-Decomposition/Physical-Optics Analysis of Finite Reconfigurable Reflectarrays Using Characteristic-Mode Macro Basis Functions
authors: Dijun Lin, Lars Manholm, Oskar Talcoth, Parisa Aghdam, Rob Maaskant
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter presents an efficient hybrid domain-decomposition/physical-optics framework for analyzing finite, large-scale reflectarrays and reconfigurable intelligent surfaces. The structure is decomposed into exterior and interior domains coupled through a generalized aperture-admittance formulation. The exterior response is approximated using physical optics, while element behavior is captured through admittance operators. Scalability to large arrays and many tuning states is achieved via a macro basis function expansion of the aperture current, which enables multi-state analysis by reusing pre-characterized element operators and updating only the elements that change state. Results for 8 x 8 and 20 x 20 arrays under different illuminations closely match full-wave references while substantially reducing memory usage and simulation time, demonstrating suitability for fast analysis and optimization of reconfigurable arrays.

title: CSI-Based NOMA–ISAC with AFDM for Multiple Sensing Users
authors: Enmin Lin, Jie Li, Yide Wang
publish: IEEE Communications Letters
abstract: This letter proposes an affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM)-based non-orthogonal multiple access-integrated sensing and communication scheme for uplink scenarios to overcome the limitations of channel state information-based sensing in high-mobility conditions. The framework enables a communication user and multiple sensing users to share AFDM resources via a novel pilot pattern. Furthermore, an iterative channel estimation algorithm is employed to effectively separate communication and sensing signals. Simulation results confirm that the proposed scheme significantly enhances signal separability and system performance in doubly dispersive channels.

title: Continuous-Wave THz Imaging of Moisture Distribution During Ceramic Drying
authors: Emil John Y. Magaway, Aleksander M. Cendrowicz, Zhenyu J. Zhang, Miguel Navarro-Cía
publish: IEEE Transactions on Terahertz Science and Technology
abstract: The lack of a non-contact methodology to monitor the ceramic drying process impedes the optimization of this critical production stage. Resorting to continuous-wave THz imaging, we are able to generate images of the water dis tribution within samples and, when paired with single-point dielectric property data, produce absorption coefficient maps of two ceramic samples as a function of drying time and water mass fraction of the whole system. This highlights the spatially-dependent drying time of ceramic samples due to local variation of material properties (e.g., porosity) that cannot be captured with the contact gravimetry industry standard. The THz continuous-wave system enables contactless, spatially-resolved visualisation and tracking of moisture distribution during drying, however, for quantitative interpretation of absorption coefficients complementary THz-TDS measurements are needed to provide the dielectric properties required for the transmission model. We report variations up to 1400% in absorption coefficient for different pixels at the same water mass fraction of the overall system. This work provides a proof-of-concept demonstration of a cost-effective non-contact inline monitoring system based on THz technology for use in the ceramic production process.

title: Physics-Aware Semantic Graph Classifier for Remote Sensing Images
authors: Lingling Li, Jiadong Lin, Licheng Jiao, Fang Liu, Xu Liu, Yangyang Li, Wenping Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Remote sensing scenes face the challenges of high inter class similarity and high intra class diversity in classification. Methods based on deep learning have advanced the field by learning discriminative features, but these methods have two key limitations: 1) the scope of intra-image feature extraction is limited to the planar spatial domain, mainly capturing visual features such as color and shape, while neglecting other important physical information like the vertical structure of scene inference and its frequency domain features; and 2) the lack of inter-image relationship modeling, which prevents the capture of latent semantic correlations among different scene categories. To address these issues, we propose a physics-aware semantic graph classifier (PASG) for remote sensing image classification. Firstly, we propose a height-aware spatial-frequency feature extraction module (HASF), which represents key physical attributes by integrating the vertical structure, spatial features, and frequency domain information of scenes. Secondly, we design a semantic-aware global-local encoder (SGLE), modeling the dataset as a graph structure where the physical features of images serve as graph nodes. SGLE refines the representation of each node by capturing local neighborhood structures and global contextual dependencies, leading to a robust cross-image contextual semantic representation. Experiments on four publicly available remote sensing datasets (AID, UCM, WHU-RS19, and NWPU-RESISC45) achieve optimal overall accuracies of 98.60%, 99.81%, 99.42%, and 97.55%, respectively. These results outperform state-of-the-art methods and validate the effectiveness of the proposed PASG classifier.

title: Estimating Hemoglobin Changes in Body Tissue Using Multispectral Imaging, Spectral Unmixing and Machine Learning
authors: Diego A. Polanco-Díaz, Liliana Granados-Castro, Omar Gutierrez-Navarro, Aldo R. Mejia-Rodriguez, Daniel Ulises Campos-Delgado
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This research investigates the application of machine learning-based regression models to estimate tissue perfusion parameters from multispectral imaging data. Our purpose is to establish a robust method for predicting the changes in deoxygenated and oxygenated hemoglobin at the fingertip using spectral unmixing algorithms. Multispectral images are processed to extract elementary spectral profiles or end-members and their fractional concentrations or abundances maps, providing a detailed analysis of hemoglobin distribution. The evaluation focuses on the relationship between the extracted abundances and changes in hemoglobin by using metrics, such as normalized reconstruction error and the adjusted coefficient of determination to quantify performance. As reference for validation, the estimated changes in deoxygenated and oxygenated hemoglobin, obtained by photoplethysmography, are compared with our proposal. In addition, perfusion maps representing the spatial distribution of changes in hemoglobin at the fingertip are generated from the proposed approach. The generalization capability of the regression models is further confirmed using crossvalidation, ensuring the robustness of the proposed approach across varying datasets. The most prominent results show a normalized reconstruction error of 0.009 ± 0.010, and adjusted coefficient of determination of 0.988 ± 0.011 using linear spectral unmixing and a random forest regressor. Tissue perfusion maps provide a detailed visual representation of the changes in deoxygenated and oxygenated hemoglobin on the fingertip, marking a significant advancement in perfusion monitoring.

title: Short-Term Forecasting of Dissolved Gas in Transformer Oil via Bidirectional Markov Imputation and EWT-Driven GBRBFNN
authors: Chunjia Gao, Deshen Li, Peng Zhang, Hanlin Li, Ruoyu Zhang, Bo Qi, Chengrong Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Accurately predicting dissolved gas concentration in transformer oil is crucial for condition monitoring and early fault warning. However, online dissolved gas analysis (DGA) data often suffer from incompleteness, high noise, and nonlinear multi-scale dynamics. This paper proposes a two-stage framework to improve data quality and enable short-term prediction. First, a bidirectional Markov transition model with an adaptive weight adjustment mechanism reconstructs missing values, preserving both local temporal evolution and global statistical features. Then, the completed time series is decomposed via empirical wavelet transform (EWT), and each component is predicted using a gradient-boosted radial basis function neural network (GBRBFNN). The final prediction is obtained by reconstructing all predicted components. Validation on field data shows an average relative error of 1.76% for data reconstruction while retaining statistical characteristics. Under normal operating conditions (400 hydrogen samples), the proposed EWT-GBRBFNN model achieves a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 1.70%, reducing prediction error by 17.1% to 51% compared with other five benchmarks. Under abnormal fluctuation conditions (168 ethylene samples), the model attains a MAPE of 2.9%, with error reductions of 64.8% to 78% and a coefficient of variation below 4.5%. The framework provides reliable data completion and accurate predictions for transformer condition monitoring.

title: Reinforcement Learning-based Sequential Route Recommendation for System-Optimal Traffic Assignment
authors: Leizhen Wang, Peibo Duan, Cheng Lyu, Zhenliang Ma
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Modern navigation systems and shared mobility platforms increasingly rely on personalized route recommendations to improve individual travel experience and operational efficiency. However, a key question remains: can sequential route recommendations issued by a centralized platform collectively drive the system toward the theoretical system optimum (SO)? This paper addresses this question by proposing a learning-based framework that reformulates the static SO traffic assignment problem as a single-agent deep reinforcement learning (RL) task. A central agent sequentially recommends routes to travelers as origin-destination (OD) demands arrive, to minimize total system travel time. To enhance learning efficiency and solution quality, we develop an MSA-guided deep Q-learning algorithm that integrates the iterative structure of traditional traffic assignment methods into the RL training process. The proposed approach is evaluated on both the Braess and Ortúzar–Willumsen (OW) networks. Results show that the RL agent converges to the theoretical SO solution in the Braess network and achieves only a 0.35% deviation in the OW network. Further ablation studies demonstrate that the route action set’s design significantly impacts convergence speed and final performance, with SO-informed route sets leading to faster learning and better outcomes. This study establishes an theoretically grounded framework that demonstrates the feasibility of aligning centralized sequential recommendations with SO traffic assignment, thereby connecting reinforcement learning methods with classical transportation optimization.

title: Flexible Physical Camouflage Generation Based on a Differential Approach
authors: Yang Li, Haonan Li, Wenyi Tan, Tingrui Wang, Quan Pan
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Achieving physical-world attacks on object detectors often relies on adversarial camouflage, which applies textures to target surfaces. However, existing methods typically simulate environmental variations through post-rendering image transformations, which do not fully account for the 3D object’s geometry and its interaction with lighting. To address this, we propose the Flexible Physical-camouflage Attack (FPA), a framework that integrates a differentiable 3D renderer with comprehensive, multi-parameter environmental randomization such as lighting, material, and viewpoint directly into the optimization loop. This ensures that the generated textures are robust to real-world variations. For texture generation, FPA leverages a denoising diffusion probabilistic model whose generative prior helps produce structurally coherent and visually realistic patterns. These are jointly optimized with a set of task-oriented loss functions including adversarial, smoothness, non-printability, and concealment constraints within the unified framework. Through systematic ablation and extensive physical experiments on a 1:24 scale model, we demonstrate that FPA achieves a high attack success rate (ASR) and strong transferability to black-box detectors. Crucially, our analysis reveals a controllable trade-off between adversarial effectiveness and visual stealth, validated by perceptual metrics and human evaluation. Our findings highlight the importance of integrated physical modeling and systematic evaluation for advancing physically realizable adversarial camouflage.

title: VN-YOLO: Detail-Preserving and Recalibrated Multi-Scale YOLO for Small Objects in UAV Imagery
authors: Ngoc-Tuan Hoang, Cao-Phuc Ha, Phu-Nguyen Le
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) have fundamentally altered many fields due to their flexibility and ability to reach inaccessible areas. They are used in military reconnaissance, agricultural operations, disaster response, environmental monitoring, and traffic surveillance. Nevertheless, identifying diminutive objects in UAV imagery remains a persistent challenge due to low resolution, complex backgrounds, and hardware limitations. Conventional object detection algorithms, including two-stage approaches such as Faster R-CNN and one-stage detectors such as YOLO, often face challenges in these scenarios, particularly when real-time performance is essential. Consequently, this study presents VN-YOLO, a modified version of YOLOv11n, specifically designed to detect small objects in drone imagery. The contributions include: (1) a detail-preserving backbone that incorporates SPDConv (Space-to-Depth Convolution), Learnable Adaptive Downsampling (LAD), RepVGG, SwinTransformer, and SPPF_LSKA for enhanced feature extraction; (2) a robust neck with C2fFast, CBAM/RFA attention, ReCalibrationFPN, AMFE, and DCNv3 for multi-scale fusion and deformation handling; and (3) a stable detection head with channel alignment and MPDIoU loss for improved localization. Empirical findings derived from theVisDrone dataset demonstrate that VN-YOLO attains a mAP50 of 0.52 and a mAP50:95 of 0.356, surpassing the baseline YOLOv11n (mAP50 of 0.3314) by 57%, all the while preserving computational efficacy. Thus, this study advances real-time small-object detection in UAV applications by introducing a model that balances accuracy and efficiency.

title: A Root Prediction System for Single-Variable Equations with Existing Taylor Polynomials
authors: Seokjun Jeong, Yoosoo Oh
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Root finding is a fundamental problem in numerical analysis with applications across scientific computing, control systems, and optimization. Classical iterative algorithms such as Newton-Raphson, bisection, and secant methods are highly sensitive to initial values and may fail to converge reliably for nonlinear equations, particularly when encountering zero derivatives, ill-conditioned Jacobians, or complex nonlinear structures. To address these limitations, we propose a direct root prediction framework that reformulates root finding as a structured prediction problem. The proposed system consists of four stages: (1) a Transformer-based interval predictor identifies the top-25 candidate intervals likely to contain real roots, (2) variable shifting and 25th-order Maclaurin expansion generate local polynomial representations, (3) coefficient-based neural models generate multiple candidate roots from Taylor coefficients, and (4) residual-based selection and validation identify the final numerically reliable root. Experiments on 10,000 test equations spanning 12 function families show that the proposed framework achieves strong performance under a strict residual threshold of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−10</sup>. Additional operating-point analysis indicates that using a 25th-order local expansion together with top-25 candidate intervals provides the most stable overall trade-off between success rate and computational cost across the evaluated model variants. Under the limited-prior-information evaluation setting adopted in this study, all proposed variants show competitive performance relative to the baseline numerical solver while producing validated residuals on the order of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−12</sup> to 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−11</sup> under the adopted evaluation protocol. Further analysis indicates that the observed performance ceiling is primarily governed by representation-level limitations of truncated Taylor expansions rather than by deficiencies of a specific prediction model.

title: Development of a Traffic Fingerprinting-based Hidden Service Access Identification System for Dark Web Drug Investigations
authors: Sol Gyu Park, Jiyeon Kim
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Advances in cryptographic technologies for information security have led to the emergence of anonymous communication systems such as Tor. While the Tor network contributes to privacy protection and freedom of expression, it is also exploited for illicit activities, including drug trafficking, child sexual exploitation, and illegal financial or weapons transactions. Consequently, investigative agencies require new technical approaches capable of identifying user access behaviors within anonymous networks. This study proposes a system for identifying connections to dark web drug marketplaces based on Tor network traffic analysis. The proposed system leverages raw traffic obtained from network bridge segments accessible to investigators and incorporates a realistic data preprocessing pipeline consisting of Tor Metrics–based filtering, user–operator distinction, and guard-node–level traffic separation. From the refined traffic, we extract core features—Burst-Cycle, Distribution-Entropy, and TCP/TLS sequence patterns—and transform them into fixed-length vectors suitable for machine learning. We incrementally increased the number of identifiable dark web sites to compare machine learning and deep learning models. Among the evaluated models, the CNN classifier achieved the highest performance, maintaining an accuracy of approximately 0.90 in the seven-class classification setting. These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework under the evaluated experimental setting and suggest its potential usefulness for inferring a suspect’s access to monitored dark web drug sites.

title: Deep Learning-Based Estimation of Ground Reaction Forces in Parkinsonian Gait Using an Optimized Set of IMU Data
authors: Run Lin, Yingtian Tang, Jiawen Xu, Dongfei Huo, Lefan Wang, Helen Dawes, Dominic J. Farris, Dong Wang, Xijin Hua
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Accurate gait analysis in Parkinson’s disease (PD) typically relies on laboratory-based systems to capture biomechanical data, such as ground reaction forces (GRFs). Estimating GRFs using inertial measurement units (IMUs) provides a feasible alternative. However, this approach remains challenging in pathological gait like PD due to its high variability and complexity. Moreover, existing monitoring approaches often require multiple body-mounted sensors, which limit practicality and reduce patient compliance. To date, no study has investigated the application of deep learning approaches to address this challenge. This study proposes, for the first time, a deep learning framework to estimate bilateral vertical GRFs (vGRFs) in PD using an optimized set of wearable IMUs. A hybrid CNN-BiLSTM model was trained separately on data from 61 PD patients and 65 healthy controls (HC) using 13 IMUs. The model achieved high intra-subject accuracy (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.98) and strong inter-subject generalization (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.93 for HC, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.91 for PD). Sensor configuration was found to significantly influence estimation accuracy, with optimal sensor placement varying between PD patients and HC. For PD patients, estimation accuracy dropped markedly when reducing to a single IMU. The optimal configuration for PD used four IMUs. We identified a minimal setup with only two IMUs still enabled robust estimation. This compact setup offers a practical and scalable solution. Overall, the proposed approach supports the development of wearable vGRF-based gait analysis systems for Parkinsonian gait and potentially other pathological conditions, enabling accessible clinical assessments, remote monitoring, and personalized rehabilitation.

title: GRADRobot: Geometry-Aware Rendering with Articulation and Diffusion for Robot Modeling
authors: Yunlong Li, Boyuan Chen, Chongjie Ye, Bohan Li, Zhaoxi Chen, Shaocong Xu, Hao Tang, Hao Zhao
publish: 2026 International Conference on 3D Vision (3DV)
abstract: Gaussian fields are a promising representation for robot body modeling due to their differentiability and inherently low sim-to-real gap. However, existing methods like Dr-Robot overlook explicit geometric constraints, leading to artifacts under novel poses or views. Directly enforcing depth and normal supervision on articulated Gaussians is unstable due to entanglement between pose deformation and 3D appearance learning. To address this, we propose a two-stage training strategy: we first learn a canonical Gaussian field in a canonical pose using dense RGB, depth, and normal supervision, establishing a geometryaware reconstruction. We then fine-tune the Gaussian parameters jointly with a deformation network conditioned on joint angles using only RGB losses, ensuring consistent geometry and appearance across poses. To further mitigate rendering artifacts in novel poses and viewpoints, we integrate a diffusion-based refinement module. This module conditions on both the initial Gaussian renderings and the target robot skeletons, and significantly enhances visual fidelity while preserving pose accuracy. Experiments across multiple robotic platforms show that GRADRobot outperforms DrRobot by a large margin in both rendering quality (PSNR) and geometric accuracy (Chamfer Distance). https://github.com/liyunlooong/GRADRobot

title: State-dependent neuromodulation reveals link between online and offline corticospinal excitability
authors: Fatemeh Khademi, Alireza Gharabaghi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: The post-intervention effects of non-invasive neuromodulation techniques are critical for their translational potential in neurorehabilitation and depend on the dynamic state of neural networks at the time of stimulation. In the motor system, state-dependent interventions can modulate corticospinal excitability (CSE), but the relationship between during-intervention (online) and post-intervention (offline) effects remains unclear. We tested whether online CSE changes during state-dependent neuromodulation are associated with offline plasticity of corticospinal pathways. Healthy participants underwent five combinations of single-pulse transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) and robotic peripheral stimulation (PS) controlled by event-related beta-band desynchronization (ERD) during motor imagery (MI). We quantified the run-wise evolution of motor-evoked potential (MEP) amplitudes during the intervention and related it to post-intervention changes in plateau corticospinal recruitment (MEPmax). MI-based interventions produced progressive online CSE increases, with the strongest facilitation when TMS and PS were delivered synchronously during ERD. Only this combined intervention showed a significant association between online MEP and offline MEPmax, whereas rest conditions showed no progressive online effects and reduced offline CSE. These results support online CSE as a biomarker for refining closed-loop neuromodulation and for developing individualized dosing strategies in translational neurorehabilitation.

title: Implementation of an Uninterrupted Real-Time Wireless Thermal Pixel Data Transfer Using HTTP Protocol via Node-MCU
authors: Tofayel Islam Shohag, Siam Sufi MD Abu, Shaira Tashnub Torsa, Nahiun Lubaba Tasnim, Anasul Haque, MD. Nayeem Hossain, Sadia Nahar, Muhammad Kabir Hussain, S.M. Sojiba Jaman, Md. Abdul Muttalib Moon, Sanjoy Kumar Paul
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Thermal imaging is a technology which allows the detection of heat radiation emitted by humans, animals and objects. It is becoming increasingly popular due to its unique applications across various fields, including medical diagnostics, building automation and industrial monitoring. Even though thermal imaging has great promise, the devices available today are costly and occasionally lack sophisticated communication features. In this study, we proposed a wireless system for real-time thermal pixel data transmission by using Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) over a Wi-Fi network. The system utilizes ESP-32 and ESP-8266 microcontrollers as transmitter and receiver respectively, with an AMG8833 thermal camera that is connected to the ESP-32 for data acquisition and processing. Performance of the HTTP is compared to Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) in terms of latency and packet loss. Results showed that HTTP achieved significantly lower latencies (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.588-2.354$</tex> seconds) compared to TCP (2.328-2.497 seconds). While connections established within 5-6 seconds over 100 meters, longer distances introduced potential packet loss. At one meter range, HTTP experienced zero packet loss. On the other hand, TCP experienced only 20 lost packets out of 30. The system accurately detected and classified objects based on temperature, recording consistent readings across client and server: 29.0°C and 20.0°C (normal object), 31.75°C and 5.25°C (cold object), and 392.00°C and 45.50°C (hot object). Data logging and visualization are implemented by utilizing ArduSpreadsheet and Excel.

title: DisenTS: Disentangled Channel Evolving Pattern Modeling for Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
authors: Zhiding Liu, Jiqian Yang, Qingyang Mao, Yuze Zhao, Mingyue Cheng, Zhi Li, Qi Liu, Enhong Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Multivariate time series forecasting plays a crucial role in various real-world applications. Significant efforts have been made to integrate advanced network architectures and training strategies that enhance the capture of temporal dependencies, thereby improving forecasting accuracy. On the other hand, mainstream approaches typically utilize a single unified model with simplistic channel-mixing embedding or crosschannel attention operations to account for the critical intricate inter-channel dependencies. Moreover, some methods even trade capacity for robust prediction based on the channel-independent assumption. Nonetheless, as time series data may display distinct evolving patterns due to the unique characteristics of each channel (including multiple strong seasonalities and trend changes), the unified modeling methods could yield suboptimal results. To this end, we propose DisenTS, a tailored framework for modeling disentangled channel evolving patterns in general multivariate time series forecasting. The central idea of DisenTS is to model the potential diverse patterns within the multivariate time series data in a decoupled manner. Technically, the framework employs multiple distinct forecasting models, each tasked with uncovering a unique evolving pattern. To guide the learning process without supervision of pattern partition, we introduce a novel Forecaster Aware Gate (FAG) module that generates the routing signals adaptively according to both the forecasters' states and input series' characteristics. The forecasters' states are derived from the Linear Weight Approximation (LWA) strategy, which quantizes the complex deep neural networks into compact matrices. Additionally, the Similarity Constraint (SC) is further proposed to guide each model to specialize in an underlying pattern by minimizing the mutual information between the representations. We conduct extensive experiments on a wide range of forecasting settings with diverse state-of-theart forecasting models, and the results validate the effectiveness and generalizability of the proposed DisenTS framework.

title: Flow-Kansformer: Predicting Gas-Liquid Flow Rate with Hybrid KAN-Enhanced Transformer
authors: Haoxiang Tang, Yan Zhang, Lin Chen, Heping Chen, Yi Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Accurate, real-time prediction of gas and liquid two-phase flow rates is crucial for effective production monitoring and reservoir management. Conventional separation-based metering requires cumbersome devices and cannot support timely process control. Existing data-driven models often construct separate networks for each phase, overlooking complex flow regimes and demonstrating limited robustness to noise or well-to-well variation. We present Flow-Kansformer, a multimodal deep learning framework that integrates multistage Venturi pressure signals with electrical resistance tomography sequences. We first extract temporal features using a bidirectional long short-term memory network. Next, we refine these features with Transformer encoders, where we replace traditional feed-forward networks with Kolmogorov–Arnold networks to enhance nonlinear modeling capacity. Finally, we employ a multi-task KAN-based head to output gas and liquid flow rates simultaneously. By replacing conventional multilayer perceptrons with adaptive spline-based KANs, our architecture gains enhanced representational power through flexible, learnable activation functions. Flow-Kansformer achieves mean absolute percentage errors of 4.28% for gas and 3.75% for liquid on nine months of field data from Well A. We successfully adapt the model to Well B within days through incremental learning. These results demonstrate a robust, accurate, and efficient solution for industrial multiphase flow rate measurement.

title: Accurate Wi-Fi RTT indoor positioning with NLOS mitigation by floor map: a multi-wall modeling approach
authors: Qing Liang, Guohao Zhang, Li-Ta Hsu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper first proposes a novel empirical non-line-of-sight (NLOS) ranging model, called a multi-wall model (MWM), for accurate Wi-Fi round-trip-time (RTT) indoor positioning under NLOS conditions. The model is designed to account for NLOS ranging biases due to through-wall signal propagation in complex indoor environments. It requires a 2D geometry floor map and learns model parameters from a few on-site training data. We present a model-fitting technique based on least-squares optimization to ease this process. Using the multi-wall ranging model, we also develop a particle-filter-based RTT positioning method that integrates odometry, RTT ranges, and a floor map. The positioning method exploits map information twofold via map matching and NLOS ranging. The modeland method are evaluated extensively by experiments on a public RTT dataset and our private data collected in NLOS scenarios. Results show that MWM hasoutperformed the naive ranging and polynomial fitting models in NLOS scenario.Compared to a data-driven Gaussian processes regression model, it has comparable ranging performance with rich training data and much better performance with few training data. Under bad NLOS conditions and with sparse AP deployment, our positioning method, with and without map matching, has outperformed the existing particle filter methods (without using MWM). This is due to the explicit modeling of through-wall ranging biases by MWM during positioning.

title: Multimodal Fusion Network for Unbalanced Spatiotemporal Data Prediction
authors: Yuan Liang, Yunyou Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Spatiotemporal data prediction plays a crucial role in many real-world applications, such as urban planning, environmental monitoring, and traffic flow forecasting. However, spatiotemporal data are often unbalanced, with uneven distributions across both spatial and temporal dimensions, presenting significant challenges for accurate prediction. This paper proposes the Multimodal Fusion Network for Unbalanced Spatiotemporal data prediction (MFNUS), an innovative framework designed to address these challenges. First, MFNUS employs hierarchical feature fusion, which aggregates features from sparse and dense modalities by fusing information at multiple levels, enabling more effective combination of the features. Second, we integrate two plug-and-play modules: the Unified Feature Interaction Module (UFIM) and the Self-Correcting Transformer Module (SCTM). UFIM enables fine-grained interactions between different modalities through an orthogonal attention mechanism, enhancing the complementarity between modalities, allowing sparse data to gain more valuable information from other modalities; SCTM leverages self-attention mechanisms to suppress redundant features, optimizing the fused representations. Finally, we conduct extensive experiments on four real-world datasets—New York and Beijing housing prices, U.S. crop yield data, and PEMS-BAY traffic flow data. The experimental results show that MFNUS significantly outperforms state-of-the-art models on all datasets, achieving substantial improvements in prediction accuracy. Specifically, MFNUS reduces RMSE by 20.1% and 18.5% on the New York and Beijing housing price datasets, respectively, and demonstrates superior predictive capability on the PEMS-BAY traffic flow dataset, showcasing its effectiveness in spatiotemporal prediction tasks.

title: Lightweight and Explainable CNN for Accurate Multi-Species Medicinal Leaf Disease Detection
authors: Jannatul Ferdous Riva, Bibi Aysha Raha, Md Asif Shahriar Arpon, Foujia Afrose Tanha, Farzana Anjum Oishy
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Medicinal plants are very important in terms of therapeutic uses, and their yield is often at risk due to diseases. These diseases are hard to identify accurately and automatically because they have subtle differences in the textures, colors, and shapes of leaves. This study presents Light-MedLeafNet, a lightweight CNN with only 0.07 M parameters, developed from scratch for multi-species medicinal leaf disease classification. The model was trained and tested on the publicly available AIMedLeafX dataset, which included four species (Cinnamomum camphora, Terminalia chebula, Moringa oleifera, and Azadirachta indica) with 13 classes. Light-MedLeafNet reached 96-97% accuracy, matching that of DenseNet201 using much less computing power. Initial tests using confusion matrices showed correct predictions on a class-wise basis with few misclassifications, even between similar disease classes. Also, SHAP and LIME analysis revealed that the model was based on disease-related areas, which offered quality interpretability and transparency. In general, Light-MedLeafNet provides a computationally efficient, robust, and understandable way to detect diseases in medicinal plants, showing that lightweight architectures can be both highly accurate and also without impacting performance or reliability.

title: Harmonic Mitigation and Switching Frequency Reduction in LCL-Filtered Grid-Tied Converters
authors: Ali Rafiee, Yazdan Batmani, Naser Souri, Ali Mehrizi-Sani
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: LCL-type grid-tied power converters play a critical role in integrating renewable energy sources while meeting stringent power quality requirements. This paper presents a novel multivariable control strategy that combines singular perturbation theory with high-gain proportional-integral (PI) control to simultaneously address three key challenges: resonance damping, harmonic distortion reduction, and switching frequency limitations. The proposed approach decomposes the system dynamics into distinct slow and fast subsystems, enabling simplified controller design and overdamped operation without passive damping elements. A systematic design methodology ensures robust stability against parameter variations and grid disturbances while maintaining decoupled control of active and reactive power. The controller achieves superior performance with a switching frequency of only 5 kHz, reducing switching losses by approximately 50% compared with conventional active damping techniques operating at 10 kHz. Comprehensive simulation studies demonstrate an 80% reduction in total harmonic distortion (THD) of grid-injected current, with experimental validation confirming current THD levels below 3% under stiff grid conditions. The results show significant improvements in power quality and system efficiency, making the proposed method particularly suitable for medium- and high-power renewable energy applications where switching loss reduction is critical

title: Machine Learning and Cultural Heritage: An Italian Perspective
authors: Giovanna Maria Dimitri
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Cultural heritage plays a crucial role for societal identity, allowing a deep connection between individuals and their historical roots. In this context the advent of deep learning technologies has significantly influenced the preservation and analysis of cultural heritage, offering innovative and technological solutions for heritage documentation, classification, and restoration. This article presents a short review of existing deep learning methodologies applied to cultural heritage preservation in Italy. By leveraging a systematic literature search, we investigate technological solutions deployed across various Italian regions and analyze their impact on the dissemination of artificial intelligence in this field. Furthermore, we perform a geolocation-based assessment of these approaches to uncover potential correlations between technological novel solutions and regional characteristics. Our findings indicate the critical role of deep learning in modernizing heritage conservation efforts, moreover, highlighting the necessity for continued research and interdisciplinary collaboration in this domain.

title: GaussianArt: Unified Modeling of Geometry and Motion for Articulated Objects
authors: Licheng Shen, Saining Zhang, Honghan Li, Peilin Yang, Zihao Huang, Zongzheng Zhang, Hao Zhao
publish: 2026 International Conference on 3D Vision (3DV)
abstract: Reconstructing articulated objects is essential for building digital twins of interactive environments. However, prior methods typically decouple geometry and motion by first reconstructing object shape in distinct states and then estimating articulation through post-hoc alignment. This separation complicates the reconstruction pipeline and restricts scalability, especially for objects with complex, multi-part articulation. We introduce a unified representation that jointly models geometry and motion using articulated 3D Gaussians. This formulation improves robustness in motion decomposition and supports articulated objects with up to 20 parts, significantly outperforming prior approaches that often struggle beyond <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2-3$</tex> parts due to brittle initialization. To systematically assess scalability and generalization, we propose MPArt-90, a new benchmark consisting of 90 articulated objects across 20 categories, each with diverse part counts and motion configurations. Extensive experiments show that our method consistently achieves superior accuracy in part-level geometry reconstruction and motion estimation across a broad range of object types. We further demonstrate applicability to downstream tasks such as robotic simulation and human-scene interaction modeling, highlighting the potential of unified articulated representations in scalable physical modeling. Project Page.

title: Deep Unfolding for RIS Enabled Backscatter Integrated Sensing and Communication System
authors: Jinqiu Zhao, Zhiquan Bai, Jiaxin Yang, Hongwu Liu, Na Li, Dongfeng Yuan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: The integration of integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) with backscatter communication (B-ISAC) presents promising applications in the field of internet of things (IoT), enabling low-power communication and improved system sensing performance. This paper proposes a novel reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) enabled B-ISAC system, wherein the RIS facilitates the backscattering of its own information to the base station (BS) and dynamically adjusts signal reflection paths to optimize communication and sensing performance at a low cost. An optimization problem is formulated to maximize the system communication mutual information (CMI) and sensing mutual information (SMI), and a deep unfolding algorithm based on the projected gradient ascent (DUPGA) is proposed. Specifically, inspired by the iterative process of the projected gradient ascent (PGA) algorithm, we design an interpretable neural network to achieve the BS hybrid beamforming and the RIS phase-shift design. Simulation results verify that the proposed DUPGA algorithm offers significant reductions in computational complexity compared to traditional alternating algorithms, while achieving similar CMI and SMI performance.

title: Towards Enhanced Sparse-View Tomographic Reconstruction Using 3D Gaussian Splatting
authors: Aqsa Yousaf, Paul Agbaje, Afia Anjum, Arkajyoti Mitra, Habeeb Olufowobi
publish: 2026 International Conference on 3D Vision (3DV)
abstract: Sparse-view tomographic reconstruction aims to recover 3D volumes from limited projection views, but often suffers from incomplete structures and volumetric artifacts. Gaussian splatting has recently emerged as an efficient representation for continuous volumetric modeling, reducing memory cost compared to voxel grids and training time compared to implicit methods. However, existing Gaussian splatting methods for CT reconstruction struggle with needle-like artifacts in sparse-view settings. To address this, we introduce two key contributions. First, we propose a structure-aware initialization strategy that uses gradient and density magnitude from preliminary reconstructions to intelligently place Gaussian primitives in high-contrast regions. Second, we adapt the well-established Beer-Lambert law from CT physics to stabilize Gaussian splatting optimization, transforming the exponential attenuation relationship into a linear domain that mitigates vanishing gradients, and stabilizes optimization. Together, these innovations lead to sharper and more stable reconstructions, achieving average improvements of 2.32 % in PSNR and 2.41 % in SSIM while using 6.47 % fewer primitives across three standard CT datasets.

title: Welcome Message: 3DV 2026
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 International Conference on 3D Vision (3DV)
abstract: Welcome to the thirteenth edition of the International Conference on 3D Vision (3DV 2026)! In 2011, 3DIM and 3DPVT conferences merged to establish 3DV as an annual conference focused on cutting-edge research in 3D computer vision and related disciplines. Since then, 3DV has become a leading venue for advances in 3D vision and graphics, encompassing sensing, modeling, analysis, rendering, and visualization.

title: Interval State Estimation for Li-Ion Batteries Using Thermally Coupled Equivalent Hydraulic Model
authors: Youdao Ma, Zhenhua Wang, Alejandro Goldar, Michel Kinnaert, Yi Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: This article investigates an interval (i.e., set-valued) state estimation problem for lithium-ion (Li-ion) battery cells. A thermally coupled equivalent hydraulic model (TEHM) characterizes lithium exchange and thermal phenomena in the electrodes of a battery cell, which is modeled as a nonlinear system with state interval constraints. For this nonlinear system, we propose an iterative zonotope-based method to achieve accurate interval estimation and investigate its convergence. In the proposed method, we adopt a zonotopic DC (ZDC) programming (where DC stands for difference of convex) to handle the nonlinearties, and present a zonotope estimator to manage the interval constraints. We consider an interval hull-based optimization problem to facilitate the zonotope estimator design, which is physically interpretable and can be efficiently solved via linear programming. Finally, results on experimental battery cell data demonstrate that the proposed method can produce accurate interval estimation results.

title: Adaptive Inverse Reinforcement Learning Optimal for Nonlinear System via Takagi-Sugeno Fuzzy Model
authors: Wenting Song, Shaocheng Tong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Fuzzy Systems
abstract: Inverse reinforcement learning (RL) optimal control is under the framework of expert-learner, where the learner system can imitate expert system's trajectory and optimal solutions via an inverse RL algorithm and minimize the cost function, thus it can deal with optimal control problem effectively. This paper presents a fuzzy inverse RL optimal control approach for nonlinear system with partially unknown dynamics. Firstly, the controlled nonlinear system is modeled by Takagi-Sugeno (T-S) fuzzy system. Secondly, the fuzzy optimal controller and the worst-case disturbance input are given via a zero-sum game. To imitate expert system's demonstrated behavior and achieve optimization, an online adaptive inverse RL algorithm is developed. The presented algorithm consists of four synchronous neural networks (NNs): a critic NN, an actor NN, a rival NN and a state-penalty NN, which are used to reconstruct expert system's cost function, optimal controller, disturbance input and state-penalty weight. Moreover, the tuning law of critic NN is implemented by using experience replay technique, instead of persistent excitation (PE) condition. It is proved that the proposed online adaptive inverse RL algorithm can learn expert system's optimal solutions and imitate its behavior. And also the presented fuzzy inverse RL optimal control scheme can make the controlled fuzzy learner system be asymptotically stable and achieve Nash equilibrium solution. Finally, a continuous stirring tank reactor (CSTR) system is provided to illustrate the feasibility and superiority of the developed algorithm.

title: Constacyclic codes with best-known parameters
authors: Zekai Chen, Min Sha
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory
abstract: In this paper, we construct several infinite families of q-ary constacyclic codes over a finite field Fq with length n, dimension around n/2, and minimum distance at least cn/ logq n for some positive constant c. They contain many constacyclic codes with optimal, or almost-optimal, or best-known parameters. We also consider constacyclic codes of various lengths.

title: A Comparative Study of Deep Learning Models for Pancreatic CT Image Denoising
authors: Ranim Bejaoui, Rim Mahouachi, Hela Mahersia
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Low dose computed tomography is routinely used to minimize radiation exposure of patients, but it has inherent quantum noise that can obscure details in more complicated organs like the pancreas. This paper compares four modern deep learning architectures based on DnCNN, Attention UNet, REDNet and NAFNet for blocking low dose pancreatic CT images. Specifically, the models were assessed quantitatively in PSNR, SSIM, RMSE and EPI, as well as qualitatively through visual inspections of the reconstructed tissues. The results of these analyses indicated that REDNet and NAFNet performed better in general. Such results show the importance of balancing denoising strength with structural fidelity in medical imaging. Future work will focus on hybrid transformer CNN architectures and unsupervised denoising strategies that provide greater strength and generalization across a number of scanners and anatomical sites. Overall, this study represents a strong comparative baseline for low dose CT restoration and provides an avenue to improve pancreatic imaging diagnostic accuracy and image interpretation.

title: ConvNeXt-Based Classifier for Early Detection of Autism Spectrum Disorder(ASD) From a Generalized Facial Image Dataset
authors: Aishwarya Mazumder, Jannat Tohfa, Md Mahbubur Rahman
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) is a condition of neurodevelopment disorder, which is associated with failure of communication, socialization and behavior. Early diagnosis is very important to allow timely interventions and ease the longterm outcomes. The current paper introduces an ASD detection system based on deep learning models and analysis of face images, where ConvNeXt is the main part of the system. The vast, heterogeneous, and highly processed body of publiclyavailable facial images was created, divided into autistic and nonautistic groups, and filtered to guarantee the generalization to various sources. ConvNeXt was fine-tuned and tested on standard performance metrics, such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and area under the curve (AUC) and reached a 98 % classification rate. Other state-of-the-art transfer learning models such as ResNet50, DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, and MobileNetV2 were also tested to compare them with the model. Our findings suggest that ConvNeXt has better predictive performance, as well as generalization, which provides a reliable, non-invasive, and scalable AI-assisted screening of ASD. The given methodology will be able to help clinicians with proper diagnosis and planning of individual intervention.

title: Empirical Quantum Advantage Analysis of Quantum Kernel in Gene Expression Data
authors: Arpita Ghosh, Farjana Sultana, Seemanta Bhattacharjee, MD Muhtasim Fuad
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: The incorporation of quantum ansatz with machine learning classification models demonstrates the ability to extract patterns from data for classification tasks. However, to take the computational advantage of quantum machine learning, researchers have to deal with various constraints. In this paper, we focus on constraints like finding suitable datasets where quantum advantage is achievable and evaluating the relevance of features chosen by classical and quantum methods. Additionally, we compare quantum and classical approaches using benchmarks and estimate the computational complexity of quantum circuits to assess real-world usability. For our experimental validation, we selected the gene expression dataset, given the critical role of genetic variations in regulating physiological behavior and disease susceptibility. Through this study, we aim to improve quantum machine learning methods and provide useful insights into how they can help solve complex classification problems in different fields.

title: MULLER: A Multimodal Data Lake Format for Collaborative AI Data Workflows
authors: Xueling Lin, Bingyu Liu, Gao Cong
