title: An Optimized Deep Learning Model for Sleep Apnea Detection
authors: Malik Abualzait, Mahmoud H. Qutqut, Ali Ahmed, Fadi Almasalha
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Sleeping is a fundamental biological function necessary for physical and mental wellbeing and overall quality of life. Sleep disorders, such as sleep apnea (SA), cause signs like difficulty concentrating, memory lapses, fatigue, lethargy, and emotional instability. SA has attracted significant research interest, with many studies using Machine Learning (ML) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The effectiveness of such a domain can be enhanced via optimization techniques. This study uses a CNN with Gray Wolf Optimization (GWO) to optimize input features and enhance diagnostic performance. Our method is benchmarked against existing approaches. Results showed an accuracy of 98.68%, a specificity of 97.92%, and sensitivity of 98.24%, with improvements of 0.12%, 0.11%, and 0.10%, respectively. These gains were achieved at the cost of a 9% increase in processing time, indicating a tradeoff between accuracy and computational efficiency. The approach showed promise for integration into wearable ECG monitors for at-home SA screening.

title: Transparent OCR Pipeline for Single-Word Recognition with Hierarchical Segmentation, Deskewing, and OCR-Aware Spell Correction
authors: Md Moshahed Parvez, Nafis Ahmed, Nawshin Nawar Nijhum, Md. Rahinur Rahman, Sad Sami
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Optical Character Recognition (OCR) remains challenging on handwritten word images due to noise, skew, and visually confusable characters. This paper presents a modular and transparent framework for single-word recognition, emphasizing interpretability over heavy machine learning. The system integrates illumination correction, adaptive medianbased binarization, hierarchical row/column segmentation, and shear-based skew correction with safeguards against degenerate cases. Characters are extracted via connected-component analysis, size-filtered, normalized to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$128 \times 128$</tex> pixels, and recognized using template correlation against exemplars from the NIST Special Database 19 (SD19). Post-recognition, a dictionary-guided spell corrector tailored for OCR noise resolves frequent confusions (e.g., <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0 \leftrightarrow O, F \leftrightarrow E, B \leftrightarrow R$</tex>) through prioritized substitutions and weighted edit-distance search. Experiments on 240 self-written handwritten word images yielded a Word Recognition Rate (WRR) of 79.6 %. At the character level, accuracy improved from 81.4 % to 93.1 % after spell correction, while the Character Error Rate (CER) dropped from 18.6 % to 6.97 %. Comparative tests show that the proposed system performs at a similar level to baseline CNNs and modern OCR transformers trained on the same dataset, while maintaining transparency and lightweight implementation in MATLAB. The results confirm that rule-based OCR, when carefully engineered, remains a practical baseline for reliable single-word recognition.

title: −188.4-dBc/Hz FoM Complementary VCO Employing Current Harmonic Cancellation Achieving 5-dB Phase Noise Reduction at 1/ f 3 Region
authors: Aulya Sholehah Wataawa Sau, Hapsah Aulia Azzahra, Muhammad Fakhri Mauludin, Youngwoo Ji, Xi Zhu, Jae-Won Nam, Jusung Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: A harmonic cancellation technique based on a phase manipulation technique is presented for Ku-band voltage-controlled oscillators (VCO), achieving significant phase noise reduction, especially at the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1/f^{3}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> region. The proposed VCO incorporates an auxiliary PMOS transistor with dynamically controlled biasing to generate additional high-order components to compensate for the intrinsic phase difference between the high-order harmonic components of the main PMOS and NMOS cross-coupled transistors, establishing the required <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$180^{o}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> phase difference (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta \theta $</tex-math> </inline-formula>) between them. The alignment, facilitated by the proposed technique, significantly suppresses the undesired high-order harmonic components entering the tank’s capacitive path and disturbing its natural frequency. The proposed VCO is fabricated in 65nm CMOS technology, achieving a phase noise of −33.78 dBc/Hz at 1 kHz and -112 dBc/Hz at 1 MHz. The phase noise improvement reaches 5.56 dB at the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1/f^{3}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> region compared to a conventional complementary VCO. With a low power consumption of 4.5 mW, the proposed VCO achieves an excellent figure-of-merit (FoM) of −188.4 dBc/Hz with a tuning range of 1.1 GHz (8.1%) operating from 13 GHz to 14.1 GHz.

title: Unified Geometry and Color Compression Framework for Point Clouds via Generative Diffusion Priors
authors: Tianxin Huang, Gim Hee Lee
publish: 2026 International Conference on 3D Vision (3DV)
abstract: With the growth of 3D applications and the rapid increase in sensor-collected 3D point cloud data, there is a rising demand for efficient compression algorithms. Most existing learning-based compression methods handle geometry and color attributes separately, treating them as distinct tasks, making these methods challenging to apply directly to point clouds with colors. Besides, the limited capacities of training datasets also limit their generalizability across points with different distributions. In this work, we introduce a test-time unified geometry and color compression framework for 3D point clouds. Instead of training a compression model based on specific datasets, we adapt a pre-trained generative diffusion model to compress original colored point clouds into sparse sets, termed 'seeds', using prompt tuning. Decompression is then achieved through multiple denoising steps with separate sampling processes. Experiments on objects and indoor scenes demonstrate that our method has superior performances compared to existing baselines for the compression of geometry and color. Our codes would be released at https://github.com/Tianxinhuang/DiffCom.git.

title: Joint Deployment, User Association, and Power Allocation for Data Collection in UAV-Assisted Wireless Sensor Networks
authors: Yanping Liu, Kunkun Zhang, Xuming Fang, Ming Xiao, Fuhong Song, Qing Xue, Yaping Cui, Changfeng Ding
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: In recent years, uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) have become increasingly prevalent for collecting environmental data from various wireless sensors. However, existing research on employing UAVs to collect data from wireless sensors has often ignored the heterogeneous requirements of sensors. In this paper, we investigate joint deployment, user association, and power allocation for data collection in the UAV-assisted wireless sensor network to accommodate the heterogeneous requirements of sensors, where a novel satisfaction function is designed for three types of sensors, including sensors with delay requirements, sensors with energy consumption requirements, and sensors with both delay and energy consumption requirements. Leveraging the satisfaction function, we formulate the optimization problem aimed at jointly optimizing the positions of UAVs, the association between sensors and UAVs, and the power allocation of sensors to maximize overall satisfaction of sensors. In order to effectively address the considered problem, we decompose it into two subproblems, i.e., joint UAV deployment and user association subproblem, and transmission power allocation subproblem. An enhanced human evolutionary algorithm is developed to tackle the joint UAV deployment and user association subproblem, and the Lagrange dual method and gradient descent method are employed to solve the transmission power allocation subproblem. The suboptimal solution is achieved by iteratively addressing the two subproblems until convergence of the proposed enhanced Lagrange and gradient descent-based human evolutionary optimization algorithm is attained. Extensive simulations demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm in enhancing overall satisfaction of sensors, underscoring its significant advantages in managing heterogeneous network environments.

title: CALSI: Context-Aware Layer Skipping Inference for On-Device LLM Serving
authors: Puhan Luo, Mu Yuan, Ningkang Zhang, Ruiguang Zhong, Junyang Zhang, Chengchen Hu, Xiang-Yang Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: The large language model (LLM) based on the Transformer architecture and its derived various applications have greatly changed people's lives. Considering some concerns such as privacy and network conditions, deploying LLM on smart devices has gradually become a research focus. In order to reduce the huge computation and storage overhead of LLMs, many works have studied model compression technology to reduce the model computation and parameter amount, thereby reducing the inference latency. This paper analyzes the characteristics of the on-device LLM service, including small batch size and latency focus, etc. We find the inefficiency of existing model compression technologies and new optimization opportunities, i.e., allocating different layers for different input tokens based on the task QoS requirements. Then we propose CALSI, a context-aware layer skipping LLM inference system for on-device serving. In the offline profiling phase, we analyze the importance of different layers of the model to different tokens and train a lightweight gated predictor. Then, we map the latency QoS requirements of different tasks with the predictor threshold. During the online inference phase, we adaptively allocate the layers that need to be computed to the specific token and design a KV cache delayed computation management mechanism to solve the KV cache missing problem caused by layer skipping. Experiments on real devices show that CALSI can achieve up to 24.1% latency reduction. CALSI has good generalization ability on various models and datasets and is compatible with other existing complementary optimization techniques.

title: A Real-Time Sign Language Translator on Jetson Nano Using Mediapipe, MobileNet-V2 and LSTM
authors: Md. Yusuf, Takia Nawrin, Maftun Ahommed, Nafees Mansoor
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: The inability to communicate verbally isolates millions of deaf and hard-of-hearing people worldwide. The World Health Organization reports that more than 466 million people worldwide have disabling hearing loss. Sign languages are the primary means of communication for these people, but the lack of widespread sign-language literacy creates significant barriers in social, educational and professional settings. This article presents a complete design, implementation and evaluation of a real-time sign-language translation system running on an NVIDIA Jetson Nano. The system captures hand gestures with an on-board camera, extracts 3-D hand landmarks using Mediapipe Holistic, produces compact spatial features via a pre-trained MobileNet-V2, models temporal dynamics with a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network and converts recognized signs into text. Comprehensive evaluation demonstrates that the proposed system achieves 98% accuracy on American Sign Language (ASL) letters, runs at 25–30 frames per second on Jetson Nano and consumes under 10W of power. The combination of efficient hardware and lightweight deep learning makes the proposed system a practical bridge between sign-language users and the broader population.

title: An Advanced Kronecker Product-Based Time-Delay Neural Network for Radio Frequency Power Amplifier Digital Predistortion
authors: Jianfeng Shao, Xi Hong, Wenjie Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: Neural networks (NNs) have emerged as the most prevalent and critical solution for digital predistortion (DPD) of radio frequency (RF) power amplifiers (PAs) in recent research. Existing schemes of DPD NNs can be categorized into two classes: those whose input datasets incorporate nonlinear characteristics derived from the memory polynomial (MP), and those that integrate a functional phase recovery block (PRB). These mainstream approaches of DPD NNs have been validated to be effective in both simulation studies and practical engineering implementations. However, existing schemes of DPD NNs lack a tailored fitting strategy for the inherent nonlinear characteristics of PAs, resulting in slow convergence and failure to achieve the desired performance metrics. To address this limitation, this article proposes an advanced Kronecker product-based time-delay NN (AKPTDNN). The proposed NN is built upon the block-oriented time-delay NN (BOTDNN) and the Kronecker product (KP)-based modules. It consists of multiple linear layers with nonlinear activation and KP operations, which are specifically designed to construct nonlinear characteristics in the form of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$x(n{\,}-{\,}m_{1})|x(n{\,}-{\,}m_{2})|^{p}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, which is based on the basis function of generalized MP (GMP) model. Furthermore, unlike KP-based modules, the proposed NN does not pass the terms, which contain the phase characteristics, through nonlinear activation functions, thus avoiding the corruption of phase characteristics. These design measures are intended to reduce the fitting burden on the NN, the effectiveness of which has been verified via NN training experiments. Performance validation using orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) signals with varying bandwidths demonstrates that AKPTDNN achieves faster convergence than the two reference schemes of DPD NNs, as quantified by the average normalized mean square error (NMSE) and adjacent channel power ratio (ACPR). Furthermore, comparative analysis of the performance distribution reveals that AKPTDNN exhibits a more stable performance range, indicating that it is less sensitive to NN initialization parameters.

title: EdgeCaps: Pruned Capsule Networks for RF Vital Sign Measurement in Real-time
authors: Prisila Ishabakaki, Michael Mollel, Hira Hameed, Hasan Abbas, Muhammad Imran, Qammer H. Abbasi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Radio frequency (RF) sensing enables unobtrusive, non-contact monitoring of vital signs and has been increasingly enhanced by deep neural networks (DNNs), which excel at extracting discriminative features from complex RF signal representations. These advances have significantly improved the accuracy and robustness of RF-based health monitoring; nevertheless, state-of-the-art DNN models remain computationally intensive and unsuitable for deployment on low-cost, resource-constrained edge devices. As a result, most existing RF sensing systems offload data to high-performance platforms, leaving real-time embedded deployment underexplored. We propose EdgeCaps, a framework that compresses a high-capacity Capsule Network (CapsNet) into a compact student model optimized for edge deployment through structured pruning and knowledge distillation. EdgeCaps transfers knowledge from a 33.7 M-parameter teacher to a 1.59M-parameter student, achieving 95.5% accuracy–within two percentage points of the teacher, while reducing parameters and computational complexity by 95%. We validate the system through continuous respiration monitoring on a Raspberry Pi 3, where the student model achieves mean inference latency below 0.3 s, RAM usage of 18%, and core temperature under 44°C, compared with the teacher’s 0.4–0.5 s latency, 22% RAM usage, and 51°C. These results demonstrate that EdgeCaps enables real-time, energy-efficient, and thermally stable RF-based vital sign monitoring directly on embedded devices. By combining CapsNet’s representational power with model compression and knowledge distillation, this work establishes a novel pathway toward scalable, low-cost, and intelligent health monitoring systems at the edge.

title: Comparative Study of Parkinson's Disease Detection from Hand-Drawn Patterns Using Dual-Input Efficientnet and Custom CNN
authors: Meherin Tasfia, Abdullah Al Numan, Minhaj Ahmed Habil
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Parkinson's Disease (PD) is a common neurodegenerative disease affecting significantly motor functions of older people. It affects the nervous system which leads to impaired movement and thus hand writing. PD affected people tend to exhibit lower pressure and velocity while writing or sketching. This phenomenon can be observed by collecting spiral and wave drawings from both PD affected and healthy controls. This work highlights the potential of automated, non-invasive screening tools for early-stage Parkinson's diagnosis, offering promise for clinical decision support and improved patient outcomes. This paper presents a novel approach for early Parkinson's disease (PD) detection utilizing deep learning techniques on spiral and wave drawing data. Leveraging the EfficientNet convolutional neural network architecture, the proposed model effectively extracts key features from drawing tests, capturing subtle motor impairments characteristic of PD. Experimental evaluation on benchmark datasets demonstrates that the EfficientNet-based classifier achieves high accuracy, precision, and F1-score in distinguishing Parkinson's patients from healthy controls.

title: Recognizing Excellence at Society Conferences [From the Editor’s Desk]
authors: David B. Durocher
publish: IEEE Industry Applications Magazine

title: Federated Temporal Collaborative GAN for Electricity Theft Detection with Imbalanced Data
authors: Pengcheng Xia, Jun Li, Zhen Mei, Songwen Xu, Yiyang Ni
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Electricity theft poses a significant challenge to power grid sustainability, emerging as one of the primary sources of energy loss in the Energy Internet of Things (IoT) systems. Electricity theft detection in smart-meter systems is increasingly benefiting from advanced digital technologies and intelligent analytical methods. However, accurately detecting these anomalies remains a challenging task. This difficulty stems primarily from the inherent complex temporal correlations in power consumption, coupled with the severe class imbalance of data among distributed users. Existing methods for detecting electricity theft in class-imbalanced data have not fully leveraged the temporal correlations inherent in data and cannot protect the data privacy of different users. To address these limitations, we propose a federated temporal collaborative generative adversarial network (Fed-TCGAN). This approach introduces a time-series prediction network as a supervisor to strictly regularize data generation. Concurrently, we embed a classifier within the adversarial architecture, sharing feature representations with the discriminator to enable collaborative training. To resolve inherent optimization conflicts, we further design a classification-generation synchronization mechanism to coordinate gradient feedback. Ultimately, this aligned architecture enables the generator to synthesize high-fidelity samples that accurately reflect the underlying distributions, substantially enhancing electricity theft detection accuracy under severe data imbalance. Experimental results demonstrate that Fed-TCGAN achieves F1-score improvements of 5.02%−14.58% over advanced baselines on the dataset with an imbalance ratio of 40 : 1.

title: Combating Visual Misinformation: Automated Detection of Deceptive Chart Design Using Vision Transformers
authors: Konrad J. Maciborski, Karol Struniawski, Adam Wierzbicki
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Deception in chart design is an insidious and effective corner case of visual misinformation: instead of distorted facts, deceptive interpretations emerge from particular choices in visual encoding. This paper investigates automated detection of deceptive data visualizations and presents a dedicated dataset covering multiple categories of visual deception. We further demonstrate that modern vision transformer architectures are highly effective for this task. We provide an annotated dataset based on different types of deception, including axis truncation, dual axes, 3D distortion, missing scales, and trend obfuscation over deceptive and non-deceptive versions of the same data. We employ a large-scale, self-supervised, pretrained, and fine-tuned DINOv3 Vision Transformer to capture fine-grained visual cues and general chart structure. Experimental results demonstrate the model reaches a macro F1-score and balanced accuracy of 98.09% on a held-out test set, outperforming convolutional and vision-language baselines.We also perform ablation studies, robustness evaluations, fairness analyses, and explainability evaluations. The results show that the model attends to semantically meaningful chart elements such as axes, scales, and data markers, providing interpretable and reliable predictions. Together, these findings establish a scalable and effective foundation for automated detection of deceptive data visualizations. To support reproducibility and future research, the HVPD dataset is publicly available at: https://huggingface.co/datasets/WieszczUrsynowa/HVPD-HarmfulVisualPlotsDataset.

title: Investigation on the Equilibrium Law and Equilibrium Coefficient Model of Methanol in Oil-Paper Insulation of Transformer
authors: Song Cheng, Hangxu Li, Wei Sun, Yun Chen, Wei Lu, Liang Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: The equilibrium behavior of methanol (MeOH) between the oil and paper phases plays a critical role in evaluating the aging state of oil–paper insulation. Existing studies generally assume a constant oil–paper equilibrium coefficient for MeOH; however, this assumption neglects the effects of insulation paper aging on its microstructure and chemical properties. In this study, eight insulation paper samples with different aging degrees were prepared to investigate the variation in MeOH equilibrium behavior. Experimental results show that aging significantly alters the paper microstructure and reduces hydroxyl group content, leading to a substantial reduction in MeOH adsorption capacity. Specifically, the adsorption capacity of aged paper decreases by up to 90.22% compared with that of new paper. Based on these findings, a variable equilibrium coefficient model dependent on the degree of polymerization (DP) is proposed. Furthermore, thermal aging experiments demonstrate that when the DP decreases to approximately 460, the total MeOH concentration declines due to esterification reactions. These results demonstrate the proposed model and provide improved support for accurate assessment of oil–paper insulation aging.

title: Noninvasive Voltage Measurement for Transmission Lines and Substations Using Lightweight Monitoring Devices Based on Torsion-Mirror Electric-Field Sensors
authors: Kun Ke, Liuting Yang, Ziheng Yao, Chengbo Hu, Zhen Wang, Yanjie Hu, Qing Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: To address the demand for miniaturized and easily deployable nonintrusive voltage measurement in power systems, this article proposes a lightweight voltage measurement method and device based on a resonant torsional Micro-Electro-Mechanical System electric-field sensing structure. First, based on the spatial distribution characteristics of the electric field, a near-conductor electric-field measurement method is proposed, and a voltage reconstruction model using a multipoint differential electric-field array is established. Correction coefficients are further introduced to compensate for reconstruction errors under practical deployment conditions. A lightweight voltage monitoring device integrating a MEMS electric-field sensor, signal conditioning circuitry, electromagnetic shielding, and wireless communication is subsequently developed. The periodic modulation conversion mechanism of the torsional micromirror electric-field sensor is analyzed, and a detection system based on closed-loop gain control is proposed for real-time and accurate electric-field measurement. A nonintrusive voltage measurement experimental platform is also established. Experimental results show that the device exhibits good linear tracking characteristics over a voltage range of 5–100 kV, with maximum measurement errors for AC and DC voltages limited to 2.5% and 1.4%, respectively. Finally, practical applications on a 220 kV overhead transmission line and at a converter station are demonstrated, verifying the engineering feasibility and operational stability of the proposed nonintrusive voltage measurement scheme and supporting its application in power systems.

title: LLM-Meta-SR: In-Context Learning for Evolving Selection Operators in Symbolic Regression_supp1-3696253.pdf
authors: Qi Chen
publish: N/A
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized algorithm development, yet their application in symbolic regression, where algorithms automatically discover symbolic expressions from data, remains limited. In this paper, we propose a meta-learning framework that enables LLMs to automatically design selection operators for evolutionary symbolic regression algorithms. We first identify two key limitations in existing LLM-based algorithm evolution techniques: lack of semantic guidance and code bloat. The absence of semantic awareness can lead to ineffective exchanges of useful code components, while bloat results in unnecessarily complex components; both can hinder evolutionary learning progress or reduce the interpretability of the designed algorithm. To address these issues, we enhance the LLM-based evolution framework for meta-symbolic regression with two key innovations: a complementary, semantics-aware selection operator and bloat control. Additionally, we embed domain knowledge into the prompt, enabling the LLM to generate more effective and contextually relevant selection operators. Our experimental results on symbolic regression benchmarks show that LLMs can devise selection operators that outperform nine expert-designed baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance. Moreover, the evolved operator can further improve a state-of-the-art symbolic regression algorithm, achieving the best performance among 28 symbolic regression and other machine learning algorithms across 116 regression datasets. This demonstrates that LLMs can exceed expert-level algorithm design for symbolic regression.

title: Recognize Excellence: Computer Science & Engineering Awards
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Computer Graphics and Applications

title: Universal Analytical Framework for the Secrecy Performance Evaluation of Multi-Carrier Systems Under Practical Rate-Adaptation Constraints
authors: Miguel López-Benítez
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: The secrecy performance analysis of multi-carrier systems often overlooks important practical limitations, such as constraints on the flexibility with which data rates can be individually adapted across sub-carriers in real-world systems. In this context, this letter presents novel analytical results based on the concept of effective SNR, which explicitly incorporates such constraints. The resulting analysis is universal in nature, as it remains valid and readily applicable to any fading scenario. The derived analytical results provide a powerful and tractable framework for evaluating the impact of both the number of subcarriers and the chosen data-rate configuration on the secrecy performance of multi-carrier systems under realistic operating conditions. A key outcome of this analysis is the demonstration that, contrary to common assumptions, the secrecy performance of multi-carrier systems does not necessarily improve with an increasing number of sub-carriers; instead, when practical rate-adaptation constraints are considered, it can in fact degrade.

title: Dynamic Event-Triggered Quantized Control for Positive Markov Jump Systems Under DoS Attacks_supp1-3693759.pdf
authors: Choon Ki Ahn
publish: N/A
abstract: This article investigates dynamic event-triggered mechanism (DETM)-based output feedback quantized control designs for positive Markov jump systems (PMJSs) under aperiodic denial-of-service (DoS) attacks, with a joint design of DETM and quantization parameters. First, a novel linear co-positive structure-based DETM is proposed for networked PMJSs under aperiodic DoS attacks and output logarithmic quantization, incorporating periodic sampling to avoid Zeno behavior while reducing redundant data transmission. Notably, the proposed DETM degenerates into the static event-triggered mechanism (SETM) as a special case. Next, considering DETM inactivity during DoS attack intervals, a novel mode-dependent piecewise linear co-positive Lyapunov functional approach incorporating a dynamic variable is developed based on DoS attack active/sleep intervals. Under this framework, sufficient conditions ensuring closed-loop positivity and stochastic stability are derived for PMJSs under aperiodic DoS attacks and output quantization. Furthermore, by applying matrix decomposition techniques to design controller gain matrices, an effective mode-dependent piecewise linear programming (LP)-based output-feedback control scheme is proposed, which co-designs DETM and quantizer parameters. Compared with a traditional linear matrix inequality (LMI)-based control scheme, the LP-based scheme significantly reduces computational complexity while alleviating conservatism in design conditions. Finally, control designs under three special cases are systematically discussed, and some examples are provided to validate the theoretical results.

title: Generating Textual Descriptions for Remote Sensing Images
authors: Maryam Mehmood, Ahsan Shahzad, Amin Ullah, Farhan Hussain, Samra Nawazish, Ruhul Amin Khalil
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Generating descriptive and semantically meaningful captions for remote sensing images remains a challenging task due to the inherent complexity of aerial scenes, characterized by diverse spatial arrangements, scale variations, and ambiguous semantics. To address these challenges, this study introduces a hybrid deep learning framework that integrates a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), a Transformer encoder, and a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) decoder. In the proposed architecture, ResNet50 is employed to extract hierarchical visual features, while the Transformer encoder models long-range spatial dependencies and global contextual relationships. The resulting feature representations are refined through a fusion mechanism and subsequently utilized by an LSTM-based decoder to generate coherent and context-aware textual descriptions. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is evaluated on benchmark remote sensing datasets, namely UCM and RSICD. Experimental results indicate that the model achieves competitive performance, with BLEU-4 scores of 0.74 and 0.54, and CIDEr scores of 3.32 and 3.49, respectively. These findings demonstrate the capability of the proposed framework to generate accurate and contextually relevant captions for complex aerial imagery. The study highlights the potential of hybrid architectures in improving automated remote sensing image understanding and caption generation.

title: Electromagnetic Vibration Characteristics Analysis of Dual Three-phase Permanent Magnet Synchronous Linear Motor Considering High-Frequency Current Harmonics
authors: Yinhao Mao, Zhaolong Sun, Anmin Ding, Chuibing Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: This paper investigates the electromagnetic vibration characteristics of a slotless dual three-phase permanent magnet linear synchronous motor (DTP-PMLSM), specifically addressing the vibration induced by high-frequency switching harmonics. Unlike conventional approaches, a refined coupled-field analytical model is established, explicitly accounting for the double-sided magnetic coupling effect and the substantial non-magnetic spacing. Based on this precise field calculation, analytical derivations reveal a compound high-frequency vibration mechanism. Crucially, a vectorial phase-cancellation effect is identified, proving that the harmonic self-excitation, rather than the permanent magnet (PM) interaction, acts as the absolute dominant excitation source in this symmetric topology. To characterize the structural dynamics, an Euler-Bernoulli beam model is proposed. Furthermore, a specialized high-acceleration dynamic trajectory strategy is implemented to overcome the inherent finite stroke constraint of linear motors for valid steady-state data acquisition. Experimental results show excellent agreement with the analytical predictions. Finally, to bridge theory and application, an active mitigation strategy via Carrier Phase-Shifted (CPS) PWM is experimentally implemented, successfully achieving a 10-12 dB reduction in high-frequency vibration. This study provides a rigorous theoretical basis and practical mitigation guidelines for high-precision linear propulsion systems.

title: Vehicle Theft Detection and Prevention System in Challenging Environment Using Deep Learning
authors: Razia Sultana, Puja Mazumdar, Safwat Mukarrama Choudhury
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Vehicle theft remains a persistent problem worldwide, with developing regions such as Bangladesh dealing with unique challenges due to limited surveillance infrastructure and slow emergency response. This paper proposes a real time proactive intelligent vehicle theft detection and prevention System that leverages advanced computer vision and edge computing technologies to detect suspicious activities and alert vehicle owners instantly to prevent such incidence. The system uses the YOLOv8 deep learning model for high accuracy object detection, including license plate recognition and human activity monitoring. Using a Raspberry Pi 4 as its processing unit, it performs local inference to ensure minimal latency, data privacy, and offline operability. License plates are extracted and analyzed using EasyOCR, and ultralytics while decision logic identifies theft scenarios by correlating human activity. Upon detecting unauthorized access or suspicious behavior, the system sets off a buzzer alarm along with sending an SMS alert via the SIM800L GSM module to the registered owner. Two different dataset featuring diverse illumination, angles, and real world scenarios was used to train and test the model, achieving over 89 % mAP in license plate detection and 96.5% accuracy in human activity recognition. The proposed system is flexible, reasonable, and suitable for deployment in urban, rural, and environments with limited resources. This work presents a scalable solution toward augmenting vehicular security utilizing AI driven edge intelligence.

title: MENACOMM 2026 Authors Index
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)

title: Probabilistic Lifetime Estimation of IGBT Power Modules Under Mission Profiles: Supporting Predictive Maintenance and Functional Safety
authors: Oskars Bormanis, Janis Zakis, Frede Blaabjerg, Deniss Stepins, Leonids Ribickis
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Power modules in industrial systems are exposed to variable load and thermal stress, which drives cumulative degradation and complicates lifetime prediction. Probabilistic modeling of this degradation is essential for predictive maintenance, risk management, and compliance with functional safety standards. This paper presents a Monte Carlo–based workflow for estimating the lifetime degradation behavior of IGBT power modules by combining measured current profiles, electro-thermal simulation, Rainflow cycle counting, Miner’s rule, and global sensitivity analysis. The framework improves traditional mission-profile methods by adding uncertainty to the key thermal parameters. This makes it possible to see skewed lifetime distributions that single-point deterministic methods cannot show. Current profiles from a six-axis industrial robot are used to obtain junction-temperature traces, count thermal cycles, and generate cumulative damage distributions. Applied Sobol sensitivity analysis identifies thermal-path impedance as the dominant source of variability, providing guidance for derating and robustness evaluation. The resulting probabilistic outputs provide relative stress indicators that can support maintenance scheduling and form a basis for reliability evidence consistent with the evidence structure required by the functional safety standards family derived from IEC 61508, once calibration data are available. The framework runs efficiently on multi-core hardware platforms, produces data directly usable within digital-twin or reliability toolchain architectures, and shows that heavily loaded robot axes accumulate substantially more damage than lightly loaded ones. These findings confirm the feasibility of a workflow that bridges mission-specific operating data with probabilistic reliability modeling in power electronics. The results are interpreted as indicators of relative stress and comparative degradation, rather than as absolute lifetime or failure probability predictions.

title: Coordinated Voltage Support Control Scheme for Islanded Sending-End MMC-Wind Turbine System During Asymmetric Faults Considering Weak Grid Characteristic
authors: Jianan Yan, Xiaojie Shi, Tao Liu, Shi Liu, Zhiqiang Wang, Tianxiang Yin, Lei Lin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: The weak grid characteristic (WGC) in the islanded sending-end modular multilevel converter (MMC)-wind turbine (WT) system narrows feasible regions and causes severe terminal voltage imbalance during asymmetric faults. Such characteristics invalidate conventional asymmetric voltage support control schemes for grid-connected inverters when applied to the islanded system. Moreover, existing asymmetric ride-through (ART) control schemes for the islanded scenario neither aim to enhance voltage support nor comprehensively consider the WGC. To address these issues, this paper reveals the voltage support principle from a circuit perspective and derives analytical expressions for boundaries of the feasible regions. On this basis, a stiff region is rationally configured, and a coordinated voltage support control scheme is proposed. The proposed control scheme not only mitigates the adverse effects of the WGC, but also offers three key advantages: 1) decentralized coordination relying solely on local signals, 2) maximum voltage support point tracking of the MMC and WTs, and 3) unaffected active current injection of WTs. The superior ART performance and adaptability of the proposed control scheme are validated through simulation and control-hardware-in-the-loop experimental results.

title: Learning Compact 3D Gaussians via Feed-Forward Point Fusion
authors: Brandon Smart, Chuanxia Zheng, Iro Laina, Victor Adrian Prisacariu
publish: 2026 International Conference on 3D Vision (3DV)
abstract: We present Splatt3RFusion, a feed-forward neural network that, given a set of unposed and uncalibrated images, directly reconstructs a compact and high-quality 3D Gaussian Splat representation of a scene. Unlike prior pixel-aligned feed-forward methods that typically predict one 3D Gaussian primitive per pixel in each image - producing severe redundancy, duplication, and ghosting on one physical surface - our approach efficiently fuses points in 3D space through a multi-scale octree structure, yielding a compact and coherent representation. Built upon VGGT, a foundation model for pose-free 3D geometry prediction, Splatt3RFusion introduces a Gaussian prediction branch that infers primitive parameters using only photometric supervision. We also introduce the ability to control the number of 3D Gaussians generated at test-time, allowing for a controllable tradeoff between PSNR and the number of 3D Gaussian primitives used. The model is efficient, reducing both memory usage and rendering cost, while achieving state-of-the-art results on RealEstate10k and ScanNet++.

title: A QGI-ESO-Based Disturbance Torque Current Compensation Scheme for Electromechanical Resonance Suppression
authors: Xinyu Ji, Huimin Wang, Yun Zuo, Chunxu Lin, Xinglai Ge
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: The disturbance torque current compensation (DTCC) scheme has attracted significant attention for suppressing electromechanical resonance. However, its effectiveness is constrained by the accuracy of shaft torque estimation. Existing shaft torque observation schemes struggle to capture the oscillatory components of shaft torque accurately. To address this, this paper proposes a DTCC scheme based on a quasi-generalized integrator extended state observer (QGI-ESO). In the proposed scheme, a QGI-ESO with the capability to estimate sinusoidal disturbances is first designed. It eliminates periodic estimation errors in the shaft torque and improves the accuracy of shaft torque estimation. Furthermore, a QGI-ESO-based DTCC scheme is designed, significantly enhancing the suppression of electromechanical resonance. The proposed scheme can estimate and compensate for various disturbances in real time, whether they originate from the mechanical or electrical side. It exhibits broad applicability for mitigating electromechanical resonance in traction drive systems. Experimental results verify the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Turbo Equalization for High-Speed Passive Optical Networks
authors: Tianyuan Kong, Nebojša Stojanović, Tom Jonas Wettlin, Giuseppe Caruso, Balazs Matuz, Emna Ben Yacoub, Marvin Xhemrishi, Xianbo Dai, Stefano Calabrò, Maxim Kuschnerov, Stephan Pachnicke
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: High-speed passive optical networks (PONs) demand advanced digital signal processing (DSP) to achieve higher data rates under stringent bandwidth constraints. In this work, we present an analytical framework to estimate the theoretical turbo equalization (TE) gain for unit-memory channels of the form <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1+\alpha D$</tex-math></inline-formula>, applicable to arbitrary inter-symbol interference (ISI) levels <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula> and forward error correction (FEC) code rates. The proposed TE algorithm iteratively exchanges soft information between a log-domain MAP detector and an LDPC decoder based on the standardized (17280,14592) code. Simulation and experimental results for PAM2 and PAM4 signaling confirm significant performance improvements, closely approaching the predicted theoretical gains. We provide a combined theoretical and experimental assessment of TE gains in high-speed optical access networks, offering a practical solution to mitigate ISI and enhance throughput in bandwidth-limited PON receivers.

title: Force Control of an Underactuated Tendon-Driven Manipulator_supp1-3688030.mp4
authors: Nicolas Jacques Serge Testard
publish: N/A
abstract: This article investigates the application of force controls on a tendon-driven underactuated robotic arm. A direct use of classical hybrid force/motion control based on computed torque control can lead to instability for this type of system. Reformulating the hybrid force/motion control using the pseudo-computed torque control (PCTC) framework leads to a stable control strategy. Finally, it is shown that applying admittance control in combination with PCTC also results in stable behavior when exerting a force against a wall or in a cobotics context. These results are then validated through experimentation.

title: Multi-AUV Underwater Cooperative Target Encirclement via Quantum-Assisted Software-Defined MARL
authors: Jialu Tian, Chuan Lin, Guangjie Han, Shengchao Zhu, Zhenyu Wang, Yunpeng Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: In recent years, autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) swarms have demonstrated broad application prospects in marine exploration. This paper proposes a Quantum-Assisted Software-Defined Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning architecture (QA-SDMARL) to perform efficient decision for multi-AUV systems-driven cooperative underwater target encirclement. By integrating quantum computing and software-defined networking (SDN) with classical multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) techniques, the AUV swarm is divided into three functional layers: global training layer, local training layer, and application operation layer, to optimize task allocation and policy coordination. Specifically, the variational quantum circuits are employed to generate initial policy weights for QA-SDMARL, while the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) is adopted to enhance parameter diversity for efficient exploration. For optimizing the experience replay buffer in QA-SDMARL, this paper proposes a quantum-enhanced experience prioritization mechanism by combining quantum mutual information (QMI) and quantum entanglement strength (QES), along with an enhanced quantum annealing-based experience replay buffer update protocol. Further, this paper designs a multi-dimensional reward function for complex underwater encirclement tasks, to significantly improve adaptability and learning efficiency of QA-SDMARL in complex marine environments. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed QA-SDMARL achieves substantial breakthroughs in convergence speed, containment accuracy, and system stability, comparing to the mainstream research products.

title: Pushing physical limits and uncovering motion templates of spine-based quadruped locomotion via reinforcement learning
authors: Zhenshan Bing, Yulong Xiao, Yuhong Huang, Qing Shi, Long Cheng, Biao Hu, Gang Chen, Yang Gao, Fuchun Sun, Kai Huang, Alois Knoll
publish: IEEE Transactions on Robotics
abstract: Flexible spines are critical to the remarkable agility and speed of animals. Translating this biological advantage to quadruped robots presents a significant control challenge, particularly in coordinating the spine and limbs for maximal velocity. In this work, we utilize reinforcement learning (RL) to develop high-speed locomotion for a bioinspired mouse robot with a lateral flexible spine. The resulting controller achieves motor performance that demonstrably surpasses non-spined and model-based methods. More importantly, our analysis reveals the principles behind this performance: the emergence of two distinct motion templates. For high-speed walking, the robot learns a “whip-like” spinal oscillation to increase leg swing frequency, while for agile turning, it adopts a dynamic “bend-and-straighten” pattern. These findings demonstrate the capability of RL to not only generate high-performance controllers but also to produce emergent strategies that, upon analysis, reveal underlying principles of high-speed, spine-driven locomotion.

title: Pattern Recognition of Cardiovascular Arrhythmia from ECG Signals using LabVIEW and Artificial Neural Network
authors: Sakhawat Hossen Rakib, A. S. M. Bakibillah, Muhammad Towfiqur Rahman, A. M. A. Zaidi, Md Shafiul Alam, Tasnim Tamanna Rafa
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: This study proposes a unique method of pattern recognition and analysis of various types of Cardiovascular Arrhythmia from Electrocardiogram (ECG) signals. The analyzed features of the ECG signals are the PR-intervals, heart rates, QRS-width, QT-intervals, and the RR-intervals. Any eccentricity found in these classified features indicates the presence of a heart problem known as cardiovascular arrhythmia. The signal processing is accomplished using LabVIEW, and the extracted features were used to design a Multi-Layer Perceptron Artificial Neural Network (MLP-ANN) for the purpose of recognizing and distinguishing patterns between normal sinus rhythm and cardiovascular arrhythmia, which are Slow Term Atrial Fibrillation (STAF), Paroxysmal Atrial Fibrillation (PAF), and the Supraventricular Tachycardia (ST). The data required to train ANN was collected from the MIT-BIH database available on PhysioNet Database. This proposed method detects any early sign of classified cardiovascular arrhythmia from ECG signals by recognizing and classifying patterns of signals using state-of-the-art ANN algorithms, which can be life-saving in crucial conditions.

title: A Safety-Oriented Self-Learning Algorithm for Autonomous Driving: Evolution Starting From a Basic Model
authors: Shuo Yang, Caojun Wang, Peng Yi, Yanjun Huang, Hong Chen
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: Autonomous vehicles equipped with self-learning capabilities still face two major challenges, namely, low learning efficiency and insufficient safety guarantees, which limit their practical deployment. To address these challenges, this article proposes a safety-oriented self-learning algorithm initialized from a transformer-based basic model trained on a small set of human demonstrations. A mixed-policy mechanism is introduced to improve learning efficiency: the basic model provides informative initialization for efficient exploration, while the self-learning component improves adaptability and generalization, thereby enabling continuous policy refinement without external supervision. Furthermore, an actor approximator based on receding-horizon optimization is constructed and ensures safety by explicitly accounting for environmental constraints. Two learnable factors, the policy mixing weight <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lambda$</tex-math></inline-formula> and the adaptive terminal discretization factor <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\eta$</tex-math></inline-formula>, are incorporated to dynamically balance learning efficiency and safety. Simulation and real-vehicle experiments demonstrate that the proposed approach learns safe and efficient driving behaviors, achieving better training efficiency and stronger safety performance than state-of-the-art reinforcement learning and behavior cloning approaches. Additional comparisons with representative safe reinforcement learning methods from four categories further verify the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

title: Performance Evaluation of a Hybrid Passive-Active Wristband for Efficient Parkinsonian Tremor Reduction
authors: Adam Koubbi, Badr Boughrara, Salma Sebbar, Yassine Salih-Alj
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Hybrid wrist-worn devices that combine passive damping with active feedback control offer a promising approach to suppressing Parkinsonian tremor while maintaining energy efficiency. Although several designs have been proposed, the relationship between system parameters and overall performance remains insufficiently characterized. This paper presents an evaluation of a hybrid wristband architecture in which a tuned-mass damper and a PID-controlled actuator operate in parallel. A two-degree-of-freedom forearm model is subjected to tremor excitation, and the system is assessed across varied damping and control configurations. Performance is quantified using three key metrics: root-mean-square (RMS) tremor attenuation, actuator control effort, and total energy consumption. The evaluation framework enables systematic analysis of design trade-offs and supports the development of wearable tremor-suppression systems that balance responsiveness with power efficiency.

title: A Scalable and Latency-aware HBF Precoding Design for LEO Satellite Communications
authors: Tzu-Yi Yang, Meng-Lin Ku, Hong-fu Chou, Symeon Chatzinotas, Hua-Lung Tsai, Yeong-Luh Ueng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Hybrid beamforming (HBF) represents a highly auspicious technological advancement that capitalizes on the strengths of massive multiple-input multiple-output (mMIMO) systems operating at millimeter wave (mmWave). The low-earth orbit (LEO) satellites utilize HBF methods to achieve high throughput and global communication while maintaining minimal resource utilization. However, standard HBF precoding requires comprehensive channel state information (CSI) and singular value decomposition (SVD) operations to build the optimum precoding matrix. Due to the high resource demands on LEO satellite systems and in light of the challenges faced in previous research efforts, we present a novel method for HBF precoding. This approach utilizes the beamspace minimum mean square error (BS-MMSE) precoding algorithm, which offers two advantages: reduced complexity in precoding and elimination of the need for explicit mMIMO channel estimation. The resolution of the hardware bottleneck associated with the BS-MMSE precoding method can be addressed by employing an LDL decomposition-based matrix inversion methodology. To enhance transmission efficiency, the utilization of low-latency precoding is considered for the development of reconfigurable matrix multiplication. Additionally, this design includes the implementation of latency-aware module scheduling to achieve fast precoding. We have successfully developed a BS-MMSE precoding design for the 1024 × mMIMO LEO satellite system that is both cost-effective and latency-aware. This design has excellent hardware scalability, allowing it to accommodate different quantities of user terminals (UTs). The implementation is carried out based on TSMC's 90-nm CMOS technology. The system operating at a clock speed of 179 MHz leads to a reduction in both precoding latency of up to 51% and cell area of at least 44% as compared to the current state-of-the-art architecture.

title: A Ka-Band Eight-Stream Phased-Array MIMO Receiver With Time-Hopping Blocker Rejection for 6G
authors: Minzhe Tang, Dongfan Xu, Yi Zhang, Yuxuan Liu, Zheng Li, Yuncheng Zhang, Yilun Chen, Minghao Fan, Ziyuan Ren, Jian Pang, Dingxin Xu, Chenxin Liu, Daxu Zhang, Duo Li, Hiroyuki Sakai, Kazuaki Kunihiro, Atsushi Shirane, Kenichi Okada
publish: IEEE Journal of Solid-State Circuits
abstract: This article presents a Ka-band phased-array multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) receiver for sixth-generation (6G) wireless networks. A time-division (TD) MIMO architecture is combined with DP antenna array to support eight MIMO streams in the Ka band. The complex weights of each RF element in both polarization arrays are sequentially switched at the Nyquist rate to enable fast-stream switching for MIMO stream coexistence. The TD approach achieves full phased-array reuse and delivers high array gain and spatial selectivity for each stream at the expense of generating switching harmonics. To address this issue, a non-uniform time-hopping (TH) sequence is introduced in this article to spectrally spread the switching harmonics and mitigate the direct blocker folding. A proof-of-concept phased-array receiver is fabricated in 65-nm CMOS, integrating eight RF elements in a 7.2-mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> core area and consuming 335.2 mW. Combined with DP antennas, the phased-array receiver supports <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$4 \times 4$</tex-math> </inline-formula> TD-MIMO across both polarizations and achieves a maximum data rate of 38.4Gb/s using eight 800-MHz 64-QAM signals in over-the-air (OTA) measurements. Benefiting from the TD-MIMO architecture, the phased-array receiver achieves 0.078 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and 5.23 mW per element per stream. With the proposed non-uniform TH (NUTH) dithering, deep notches are generated at switching harmonics and 42.9-dB alias suppression is achieved. The proposed phased-array receiver also demonstrates a 34-dB improvement in blocker tolerance, maintaining –30-dB EVM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rms</sub> under a 0-dBc in-band blocker sweeping across –90° to 90° field of view (FoV) in MIMO OTA measurements.

title: Enhancing 3D Object Tracking via Dual-Context Propagation and Temporal Context Fusion
authors: Peijing Jiang, Yuanping Zhang, Jinlong Pang, Zhongjun Lin
publish: 2026 International Conference on 3D Vision (3DV)
abstract: Point cloud-based 3D single object tracking (3D SOT) plays a pivotal role in applications such as autonomous driving and robotic vision. Despite recent progress, most existing approaches rely solely on current-frame features for target localization. This approach overlooks temporal information that is crucial for robust tracking under occlusion, appearance variations, and sparse point clouds. In addition, the effectiveness of 3D SOT largely depends on the quality of feature fusion between the target template and the search region. Traditional fusion strategies often suffer from limited interaction capacity and weak discriminative representation. To address these challenges, we propose DT-Tracker, which performs multi-layer bidirectional feature interaction and temporal cue propagation to improve tracking robustness and feature discrimination capability. Specifically, we introduce a Dual-Context Propagation Network that applies bidirectional cross-attention across multiple layers between the template and search region, enabling deep semantic alignment and progressive feature refinement. Furthermore, we design a Temporal Context Fusion module that adaptively incorporates temporal cues from historical fusion features into the current frame, effectively improving resilience to occlusion and appearance drift. Extensive experiments on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets demonstrate that DT-Tracker achieves competitive results compared to existing representative methods.

title: Parametric Gaussian Human Model: Generalizable Prior for Efficient and Realistic Human Avatar Modeling
authors: Cheng Peng, Jingxiang Sun, Yushuo Chen, Zhaoqi Su, Zhuo Su, Yebin Liu
publish: 2026 International Conference on 3D Vision (3DV)
abstract: Photorealistic and animatable human avatars are a key enabler for virtual/augmented reality, telepresence, and digital entertainment. While recent advances in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have greatly improved rendering quality and efficiency, existing methods still face fundamental challenges, including time-consuming per-subject optimization and poor generalization under sparse monocular inputs. In this work, we present the Parametric Gaussian Human Model (PGHM), a generalizable and efficient framework that integrates human priors into 3DGS for fast and high-fidelity avatar reconstruction from monocular videos. PGHM introduces two core components: (1) a UV-aligned latent identity map that compactly encodes subject-specific geometry and appearance into a learnable feature tensor; and (2) a disentangled Multi-Head U-Net that predicts Gaussian attributes by decomposing static, pose-dependent, and view-dependent components via conditioned decoders. This design enables robust rendering quality under challenging poses and viewpoints, while allowing efficient subject adaptation without requiring multiview capture or long optimization time. Experiments show that PGHM is significantly more efficient than optimization-from-scratch methods, requiring only approximately 20 minutes per subject to produce avatars with comparable visual quality, thereby demonstrating its practical applicability for real-world monocular avatar creation.

title: DINO in the Room: Leveraging 2D Foundation Models for 3D Segmentation
authors: Karim Knaebel, Kadir Yilmaz, Daan De Geus, Alexander Hermans, David Adrian, Timm Linder, Bastian Leibe
publish: 2026 International Conference on 3D Vision (3DV)
abstract: Vision foundation models (VFMs) trained on large-scale image datasets provide high-quality features that have significantly advanced <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 D$</tex> visual recognition. However, their potential in 3D scene segmentation remains largely untapped, despite the common availability of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 D$</tex> images alongside 3D point cloud datasets. While significant research has been dedicated to 2D-3D fusion, recent state-of-the-art 3D methods predominantly focus on 3D data, leaving the integration of VFMs into 3D models underexplored. In this work, we challenge this trend by introducing DITR, a generally applicable approach that extracts <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 D$</tex> foundation model features, projects them to 3D, and finally injects them into a 3D point cloud segmentation model. DITR achieves state-of-the-art results on both indoor and outdoor 3D semantic segmentation benchmarks. To enable the use of VFMs even when images are unavailable during inference, we additionally propose to pretrain 3D models by distilling 2D foundation models. By initializing the 3D backbone with knowledge distilled from 2D VFMs, we create a strong basis for downstream 3D segmentation tasks, ultimately boosting performance across various datasets.

title: Robotic Welding with AI in the Loop
authors: Hamid Reza Saadati, Wojciech Kowalczyk, Sebastian Feldmann
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive overview of developing and implementing an AI-enabled intelligent robotic welding system. The primary objective of the project is to design and realize a fully automated welding solution that combines CAD-based path planning, real-time seam tracking, cloud-based data streaming, and AI-driven defect detection. The proposed system is capable of performing high-precision and consistent welding operations, thereby improving process efficiency, enhancing weld quality, and minimizing the need for manual intervention or conventional robot programming. Key components of the system include dynamic vision-based seam tracking integrated with arc sensing feedback, CAD file processing for optimized vision-based seam tracking, and cloud connectivity for remote monitoring and data analysis. Additionally, an AI model is deployed for real-time defect detection and classification, contributing to a closed-loop, intelligent welding process.

title: Hierarchical Scene Fusion for Trajectory Prediction in Autonomous Driving
authors: Hui Li, Qinghua Lin, Ye Tao, Xiangzhen Kong, Xiaoxue Ai, Fahou Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: In traffic scenarios, the trajectory of a target vehicle is influenced by the combined effects of road elements and surrounding vehicles within both its local and global driving field-of-view. The development of Vehicle-to-Everything communication allows autonomous vehicles to acquire broader dynamic scene information, but also imposes higher demands on interaction modeling. Therefore, accurately and efficiently modeling the interactions between vehicles and road information across different visual ranges is crucial for rational decision-making in autonomous driving. To provide a scalable solution compatible with such a dynamic scene, we propose Hierarchical Scene Fusion for Trajectory Prediction in Autonomous Driving. First, we propose a Local Indicator Neighborhood Attention module with relative position encoding. Based on a novel token-centric data representation, it efficiently captures social interactions between vehicles and roads within the local driving scene. Second, we propose a Global Indicator Tokens Attention module. This module utilizes Global Indicator Tokens to unify the mapping of the target vehicle's local driving field-of-view information onto the global driving scene, capturing long-range semantic interactions while overcoming information loss caused by excessive scene elements during global interaction. Finally, we propose an Agent-Specific Decoding Mechanism. This mechanism employs a dynamically weighted network to train an adaptive Bézier curve model, which regresses more accurate predicted trajectories based on the latent features of each target vehicle. Experimental results on the public Argoverse 1 and Argoverse 2 datasets demonstrate that our method achieves superior trajectory prediction performance with fewer parameters. The source code can be obtained at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/John122-art/MDC-HVA</uri>.

title: Evaluating Temporal Fusion Transformers for Short-Horizon Urban Traffic Forecasting Under Real-World Deployment Constraints
authors: Khang Nguyen, Dennis O. Xu
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Reliable short-horizon traffic forecasting is critical for urban traffic management, freight logistics, and delivery reliability. While advanced temporal deep learning models have shown strong performance on benchmark time-series tasks, their operational benefit over established industry baselines remains unclear under real-world deployment constraints. This paper evaluates whether Temporal Fusion Transformers (TFTs) provide meaningful improvements over a widely deployed gradient-boosted decision tree (GBDT) baseline for short-term urban traffic congestion forecasting and route-level estimated time of arrival (ETA) prediction. Using probe-speed roadway data from the Georgia Department of Transportation, traffic state is modeled at the Traffic Message Channel (TMC) level via a normalized speed ratio. Both models predict identical multi-horizon targets at 5,15,30, and 60 minutes and are evaluated on the same chronologically held-out test set. Segment-level predictions are aggregated into route-level ETAs to assess comparative downstream utility under a static aggregation framework. Results show that the GBDT baseline outperforms the TFT at short horizons (5-15 minutes), while the TFT achieves lower error at longer horizons (30 - 60 minutes). These findings indicate that increased model complexity does not uniformly improve near-term forecasting and is primarily justified for longer-horizon planning scenarios.

title: Classical Trajectory Planning for Dual-Camera Visual Servoing on Edge Systems
authors: Abilash Philip Madavath, Chandra Yuvesh Aubeeluck, Augustin Raju, Felix Hackelöer, Florian Zwanzig, Nicolas Pyschny
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Mechatronics and Robotics Engineering (ICMRE)
abstract: Grasping moving objects in industrial environments requires precise synchronization between perception and robot actuation. While deep learning has advanced object detection, the latency introduced by Edge AI computation can degrade interception accuracy in high-speed conveyor applications. This paper presents a comparative analysis of trajectory prediction and interception algorithms implemented on an edge platform (NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano). The proposed system utilizes a dual-camera architecture, handing over control from a conveyor overhead predictor to a local wrist-mounted visual servo. We study two prediction algorithms (RANSAC-based linear extrapolation versus Kalman filtering) and five interception strategies (Analytical Linear, Fixed-Point Iteration, Newton-Raphson, Secant, and Bisection methods) against ground-truth data obtained via an OptiTrack motion capture system. Results indicate that while RANSAC offers superior fitting accuracy, the Kalman filter provides the low-latency performance required for real-time control. Among interception solvers, the Newton-Raphson method demonstrated the optimal balance between computational speed and stability. Furthermore, we analyze the impact of adding lead time on the interception success rate. The results quantify these trade-offs, establishing design guidelines for algorithm selection in real-time dynamic picking.

title: An AI/ML Framework for Proactive Cyber Threat Detection in Uncrewed Aerial Vehicles
authors: Oludolapo. E Ogunfowora, Aarti Panday
publish: 2025 IEEE AFRICON
abstract: Uncrewed Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) have become indispensable across sectors ranging from logistics to defense, but their increasing reliance on software controlled cyber physical systems leaves them highly susceptible to sophisticated cyberattacks. Current cybersecurity mechanisms are predominantly reactive and fail to address emerging multivector threats such as GPS spoofing, sensor manipulation, and adversarial machine learning exploits. This paper proposes an adaptive, and protocol agnostic intrusion detection system (IDS) leveraging hybrid artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) techniques tailored for UAV environments. The framework integrates Kalman Filter driven anomaly detection with supervised and reinforcement learning algorithms, enabling continuous adaptation to evolving threats with minimal computational overhead. Blockchain based logging is integrated into the IDS pipeline to further strengthen resilience against adversarial attacks designed to deceive AI models. The framework is evaluated in a virtual UAV testbed replicating real-world flight and communication dynamics, using benchmark datasets (e.g., CSE-CIC-IDS2018) augmented with UAV specific attack scenarios. Preliminary results demonstrate improved detection accuracy and adaptability to novel threats, compared to conventional IDS solutions. The proposed framework has the potential to significantly advance proactive cybersecurity in UAV systems while remaining deployable on resource constrained platforms.

title: Vision-Energy Coupled Robotic Dry Ice Cleaning with Adaptive Trajectory Planning for Runway Prism Lights
authors: Kezheng Sun, Yongyong Sun, Wu Yan, Yangmin Ou, Zhaoyang Liao
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: Embedded runway lights, particularly centerline and touchdown zone lights, are critical for aircraft safety during landing and takeoff. However, the recurring accumulation of aircraft tire rubber on prism surfaces significantly degrades their optical performance. Traditional manual cleaning is laborintensive and inefficient, while existing automated systems often employ fixed-parameter cleaning, leading to resource waste and suboptimal utilization of narrow maintenance windows. This paper proposes an intelligent, vision-guided dry ice cleaning system that integrates a six-axis robotic arm with localized contamination assessment. We develop a Threshold-based Area Ratio (TAR) model to quantitatively score the cleanliness of vertically partitioned prism zones. Furthermore, an Effective Energy Flux Model (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$E_{\text {eff }}$</tex>) is established to bridge the gap between visual scores and physical cleaning intensity. Based on this, a Loop Index Compensation (LIC) algorithm is implemented for adaptive trajectory planning, enabling a “clean-on-demand” workflow. Experimental results using a ColdJet PCS 60 system demonstrate that the proposed method restores prism cleanliness to over 0.75 while reducing dry ice consumption by approximately 25 % and shortening operational time by up to 44 % compared to conventional fixed-parameter methods.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Neural Architectures for Collaborative Autonomous Intelligence
authors: Sandeep Soni, Rajvir Saini
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: HITL neural architectures have also emerged as a promising method to enhance the decision-making, flexibility, and safety of autonomous systems. Some of the difficulties that autonomous systems face are a lack of flexibility and transparency, plus the ability to adapt to unforeseeable environments. These shortcomings are especially evident in risky applications like autonomous driving, robotics, and the medical field. This study will present the new version of the HITL framework that would have assisted in overcoming these limitations by incorporating human feedback into the decisionmaking process in real-time. The architecture allows human learning through human expert participation in the strategic decision-making locations, enhanced edge case handling, and increased decision-making in dynamic and demanding environments. The system that was proposed was tested in three key areas, which were autonomous driving, robotics, and healthcare. The experimental findings indicate that the HITL system performs better than the fully autonomous system, with a decision accuracy of 92.5% as opposed to 85.0% of the autonomous system. Moreover, the HITL system demonstrates a 15% acceleration in learning rate, which is the positive influence of human feedback on the system to be able to adapt and learn. Although these results are promising, there are challenges associated with the integration of human feedback in real-time, and these challenges include feedback latency and scalability. This paper emphasizes the usefulness of HITL systems in enhancing autonomous decision-making and proposes future research directions to minimize the delays in feedback and make autonomous systems more robust and adaptable, which would result in more robust, ethical, and adaptable autonomous systems.

title: DeepLymphNet: A Deep Learning and approach for Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia Detection
authors: Minal K Deore, M Brindha
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Death due to cancer is still proceeding that lead to delayed diagnoses or missed detection. Metastasis is a significant contributor to the cancer fatality rate. Early detection of or recognition carcinogens is critical to facilitate early diagnosis and treatment. Some common cancers include breast, prostate and lung cancer and they are usually diagnosed using a variety of imaging modalities such as Computed Tomography (CT), Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) or an X-ray. Although pattern recognition techniques and diagnostic tools such as ABCD (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter) works exist these solutions might not achieve the best results. From this, there is large opportunity for healthcare automation; especially in the prediction and prevention of deadly diseases like cancer. This work presents DeepLymphNet, a concurrent deep learning based framework for classification and detection of ALL. Through employing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) using different types of activation functions such as Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU), Attention-based ReLU (AReLU), Swish, Mish and Exponential Linear Unit -ELU, the proposed model achieved a record 100% accuracy. Overall, the DeepLymphNet framework yields better results than classic diagnostic techniques and proposes a new fully automatic solution for analyzing leukocytes samples. This work shows the strong capability of deep learning techniques in medical image analysis, which provides a fast and cost-effective way to diagnosis relying on human experience that is susceptible to error. By minimising the chances of a false positive diagnosis, the new approach may speed up cancer detection and enhance survival prospects.

title: HOTP - High-conf Only Taken Predictor for Efficient Superscalar Multi-level Branch Predictor
authors: Bhawandeep Singh Harsh, Jose Renau
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on Performance Analysis of Systems and Software (ISPASS)
abstract: Branch prediction in superscalar processors faces a fundamental trade-off between prediction latency and accuracy. Ideally, a designer would like a single, very large predictor that can make multiple high-quality predictions in one cycle, but such a design only works at low frequency. Traditional multi-level or override predictors provide fast restart after mispredictions but sacrifice accuracy and effective capacity due to redundancy between the slow and fast predictors. Ahead predictors tolerate prediction latency by using older history as the predictor index. The reduced history affects accuracy, and the large predictor affects restart latency. This latency–accuracy tension limits performance in wide-issue superscalar designs. We propose a biased overriding multi-level hierarchy with three predictor levels (L0, L1, L2). L0 uses a High-Confidence-Only Taken (HOTP) predictor that only predicts high-confidence taken control-flow instructions. L1 and L2 are TAGE-based predictors sharing a bimodal table with biased updates. This design achieves fast 1-cycle restart for common branches while maintaining high accuracy for difficult predictions. Our evaluation shows that HOTP $+\mathrm{L} 1+\mathrm{L} 2$ achieves $3.4 \%$ higher IPC than TAHEAD and $3.2 \%$ higher than a traditional 2bit $+\mathrm{L} 1+\mathrm{L} 2 \mathrm{nb}$ hierarchy, demonstrating that properly designed multi-level hierarchies can surpass continuous-prediction approaches. For superscalar implementations requiring multiple predictions per cycle, we size a superscalar TAGE variant (BATAGE) using Optuna-based search with varying table sizes and aspect ratios. Combining HOTP, biased training, and this optimally sized predictor yields $3.7 \%$ IPC improvement over traditional 3-level predictors (2bit+L1+L2nb), offering concrete design options for modern open-source superscalar cores.

title: Tracked Unmanned Ground Vehicle Navigation Based on A* Algorithm
authors: Wang Wen, Liying Zhang, Siyang Wen, Yacong Hu
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Mechatronics, Control and Robotics (ICMCR)
abstract: Efficient path planning is crucial for tracked unmanned ground vehicles (T-UGVs) in complex environments, such as PV installation sites.
