title: Pretrained Biomedical Foundation Models: Comparative Analysis and Practical Applications in Healthcare
authors: Mounira Zouaghi, Fadi Kacem, Habib Fathallah
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: The rapid proliferation of pretrained biomedical foundation models has transformed healthcare AI, yet researchers and clinicians face persistent challenges in selecting the most appropriate model for a given application, as architectures, pretraining strategies, and computational requirements vary substantially across systems. This review provides a structured comparative synthesis of representative pretrained biomedical foundation models to support informed model selection for clinical and research applications. Seven models spanning encoderonly, decoder-only, and multimodal architectures and three pretraining strategies, continued pretraining, from-scratch pretraining, and instruction tuning, are compared across biomedical NLP benchmarks covering named entity recognition, question answering, and clinical reasoning. A multi-criteria framework evaluates each model across architecture, strategy, vocabulary type, task suitability, scale, and open-weight accessibility. Pretraining strategy and vocabulary alignment appear to be more influential factors in downstream performance than parameter count alone: from-scratch domain pretraining tends to outperform continued pretraining in several reported benchmarks, and instruction tuning has been shown to enable expert-level performance on specific clinical reasoning benchmarks (e.g., USMLE). These findings suggest that model selection should be guided by corpus-task alignment rather than scale, and that openweight alternatives can recover a meaningful fraction of closedmodel performance at lower deployment cost. Practical selection guidelines, current limitations including bias, hallucination, and computational accessibility, and future research directions are discussed.

title: DDAD: Diffusion in Dimensionless Domain for Generalized Hyperspectral Anomaly Detection
authors: Xiangrong Zhang, Jieqiong Du, Guanchun Wang, Shiqi Wu, Chaowei Fang, Xu Tang, Licheng Jiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Hyperspectral anomaly detection (HAD) aims to distinguish background and anomalous targets in hyperspectral images (HSIs). In real-world applications, HSIs are often acquired under varying conditions, such as different sensors, times, and locations. This makes generalized HAD particularly important for practical deployment. Most existing methods follow an unsupervised reconstruction-based framework (e.g., autoencoders, GANs) that detects anomalies by measuring reconstruction errors. However, two spectral barriers remain: Spectral Divergence and Spectral Confusion, that hinder the realization of generalized HAD: 1) cross-scene HSIs exhibit diverse spectral characteristics. The spectral divergence in the original spectral space leads to degraded generalized detection performance; 2) most methods rely on pixel-level detection in the original space, which is prone to mixed-pixel effects that hinder accurate background reconstruction. To break these spectral barriers, we propose a novel Dimensionless Diffusion framework for generalized HAD (DDAD). First, we introduce a dimensionless domain transformation module that maps HSIs from different sources into a unified, spectral-invariant dimensionless domain, which effectively eliminates spectral divergence and confusion across scenes. Next, we propose a controllable dimensionless diffusion reconstruction module that achieves balanced background reconstruction and anomaly suppression through controllable explicit background modeling. Additionally, to further enhance generalization, we design a dynamic spatial-spectral perturbation mechanism to mitigate the model’s reliance on single-source characteristics. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate the superior cross-scene generalized performance of DDAD.

title: Enhanced Stacking Ensemble Using Multi-Level Meta-Learning for Student Course Evaluations Sentiment Analysis
authors: Ramzi El Habbal, Ziad Doughan, Sobhi Abou Chahine
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Student voice is an important element in the quality assurance and enhancement of Universities; however, when it comes to assessing student course evaluations, the most common way has been through basic statistical analysis and/or manual review procedures that have trouble in capturing the complex and subtle emotional nuances rooted in student responses to course evaluations. In this study, we propose an enhanced stacking ensemble framework using seven different base models - Logistic regression with both L1 and L2 regularization, Naïve Bayes, Random forest, Extra trees, gradient boosting, calibrated support vector machine (SVM), and fine-tuned RoBERTA transformer as well as sophisticated multi-level meta learning. Advanced feature extraction techniques are employed to extract 64 meta features from course evaluations, including entropy, confidence margins, probabilities as well as VADER sentiment scoring and linguistic metrics. Class imbalance is addressed by utilizing intelligent sampling with SMOTE-Tomek linkages, and the final prediction is made by an ensemble of meta learner models (XGBoost, Light GBM, CatBoost, and logistic regression with weighted voting) that optimize the final model's predictions. Results obtained on a dataset of 5563 course evaluations comments, where each feedback was labeled with a sentiment, indicate excellent performance (accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=96.77 \%$</tex>, F1 macro <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$= 96.39 \%$</tex>, Precision <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=95.94 \%$</tex>, Recall <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=96.88 \%$</tex>), which represents a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 9 8 \%}$</tex> increase compared to the best baseline stacking approach for evaluating the sentiment in student comments. Therefore, the proposed framework can automatically analyze large amounts of comments and provide a scalable and timely solution for enhancing educational programs and improving teaching quality at universities.

title: Short-Window Gamma-Gamma Parameter Estimation With SW-GGNet for Satellite-to-Ground Laser Communication
authors: Tongfei Chen, Zhiyi Hou, Zhi Liu, Yanyan Li, Changhao Zhou
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: Satellite-to-ground laser communication requires short-window channel estimation because atmospheric turbulence can change over short time scales and render long-window estimates unreliable for adaptive transmission. This letter proposes a Short-Window Gamma-Gamma Network (SW-GGNet), a lightweight one-dimensional convolutional neural network (1D-CNN), to estimate the Gamma-Gamma fading parameters (α, β) from short irradiance sequences. SW-GGNet uses an auxiliary scintillation-index head for physics-informed regularization.With 512 samples, it reduces normalized mean squared error (NMSE) by 43%, 42%, 30%, and 48% relative to maximum likelihood estimation (MLE), the generalized method of moments (GMM), long short-term memory (LSTM), and a histogram-based CNN, respectively, and achieves a 70% reduction relative to MLE at SNR = 5 dB.

title: Explainable Kidney Stone Detection from CT Scans: Integrating Grad-CAM with YOLO Model
authors: Fatma Safi, Fadi Kacem, Walid Jallouli, Habib Fathallah
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in medical imaging has transformed diagnostic practices. However, the opacity of many AI models, particularly deep learning systems, poses significant challenges in clinical settings. Explainable AI (XAI) emerges as a cutting-edge solution, aiming to enhance the interpretability and trustworthiness of AI outputs in healthcare. In this study, we provide a comparative analysis of multiple XAI methods to assess their effectiveness in improving interpretability. Additionally, we propose an explainable framework that integrates YOLO11 with Grad-CAM to both detect kidney stones and provide visual explanations of the model's decision-making process. Quantitative evaluation demonstrated high segmentation performance with a precision of 96.6 % and recall of 97.8 %. Qualitative assessment showed that Grad-CAM effectively highlighted clinically relevant areas, increasing model transparency in real-world diagnosis. The proposed approach bridges the gap between accurate automated kidney stone segmentation and clinical explainability, providing a robust tool for computerassisted diagnosis.

title: CamC2V: Context-aware Controllable Video Generation
authors: Luis Denninger, Sina Mokhtarzadeh Azar, Juergen Gall
publish: 2026 International Conference on 3D Vision (3DV)
abstract: Recently, image-to-video (I2V) diffusion models have demonstrated impressive scene understanding and generative quality, incorporating image conditions to guide generation. However, these models primarily animate static images without extending beyond their provided context. Introducing additional constraints, such as camera trajectories, can enhance diversity but often degrade visual quality, limiting their applicability for tasks requiring faithful scene representation. We propose CamC2V, a context-to-video <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(C 2 V)$</tex> model that integrates multiple image conditions as context with 3D constraints alongside camera control to enrich both global semantics and fine-grained visual details. This enables more coherent and context-aware video generation. Moreover, we motivate the necessity of temporal awareness for an effective context representation. Our comprehensive study on the RealEstate10K dataset demonstrates a 24.09 % (FVD) improvement in visual quality and camera controllability. Our code is publicly available at: https://github.com/LDenninger/CamC2V.

title: Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning for Fraud Detection in Distributed Banking Networks
authors: Ifeanyi Bryan Uzoatu, Olamide Jogunola, Ahmed Danladi Abdullahi, Bamidele Adebisi, Tooska Dargahi
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Credit card fraud poses a critical threat to global financial systems, with annual losses exceeding $485 billion, necessitating robust detection mechanisms that balance predictive accuracy with stringent data privacy requirements. Existing approaches face fundamental limitations: Centralised machine learning models compromise regulatory compliance due to sharing the training data and model with third parties, while standard federated learning (FL) remains vulnerable to gradient-based privacy attacks. This paper presents a novel privacy-preserving framework integrating Conditional Tabular Generative Adversarial Networks (CTGAN) with Differentially Private FL. Unlike existing federated GAN approaches that suffer from training instability, our framework maintains local CTGAN synthesis whilst federating only the downstream classifier, achieving superior synthetic data fidelity. The framework employs local synthetic data generation with formal differential privacy (DP) guarantees at both the generation and the federated training stages. Extensive evaluation on two benchmark fraud datasets partitioned across three simulated banking institutions demonstrates an F1-score of 0.9485 ± 0.0014 across 10 independent runs, retaining 95.2% of the centralised-real baseline whilst providing formal (ε, δ)-DP at privacy budget (ε = 3.0) and failure probability (δ = 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">–5</sup>). Privacy evaluation against five state-of-the-art defences, confirms that membership-inference accuracy remains at chance level (0.48–0.51) across all tested configurations, and that model-inversion reconstruction error increases under DP. This work advances privacy-preserving financial analytics by providing a rigorously validated, regulatory-compliant framework enabling collaborative fraud detection across distributed banking networks without compromising institutional data sovereignty.

title: Ultra-low-power Monostatic Backscatter Platform with Phase-Aware Channel Estimation and System-Level Validation
authors: Hanyeol Ryu, Sangkil Kim
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: This paper presents a novel phase-aware channel-estimation (CE) method and an experimentally validated hardware–software platform for an ultra-low-power monostatic backscatter link. Conventional phase-stationary least squares (LS) and linear minimum mean square error (LMMSE) estimators incur deterministic bias when residual phase-slope is present across pilot symbols, a backscatter-specific impairment that is particularly consequential in monostatic configurations. To address this limitation, this paper introduces a lifted pilot model augmented with a linearized residual-phase-slope regressor, yielding closed-form phase-aware LS and LMMSE estimators with minimal additional computation. A double-Rician fading model characterizes the cascaded forward and backscatter hops, and a resource-optimal pilot allocation rule is analytically derived to balance CE accuracy against payload efficiency. The prototype integrates a semi-passive tag, a software-defined radio (SDR) reader, and a 2×1 planar Yagi–Uda array providing 7 dBi gain and over 30 dB isolation at 2.4–2.5 GHz. The receiver combines pilot-aided carrier frequency offset (CFO) compensation, the proposed lifted estimators, and zero-forcing (ZF) equalization for symbol recovery over the cascaded backscatter channel. It achieves 500 kbps at 1 m, with tag-side power consumption of 10 μW for the radio-frequency (RF) switch and 11.2 mW including the general-purpose microcontroller unit (MCU), corresponding to 20 pJ/bit and 22.4 nJ/bit, respectively. Measured error vector magnitudes (EVMs) are 2.97% for on-off keying (OOK) and 4.02% for binary phase-shift keying (BPSK). Bit error rate (BER) measurements and over-the-air (OTA) full-color image reconstruction confirm reliable multimedia-capable operation and provide practical codesign guidance for future Internet of Things (IoT) deployments.

title: Physics-Guided Surface Current Reconstruction of 3D PEC Targets from a Single Image via Cascaded Neural Networks
authors: Xiangwei Liu, Te Shi, Kuisong Zheng, Haixuan Zhang, Chaoqun Fan, Jianzhou Li, Gao Wei, Changying Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Accurate reconstruction of electromagnetic surface currents is essential for radar signature prediction, scattering-mechanism interpretation, and electromagnetic scene understanding. However, traditional computational electromagnetic methods require complete geometric models and fine surface meshing, resulting in high computational costs and limiting applicability in vision-based scenarios where only single-view images are available. This paper proposes a physics-guided cascaded neural network to infer the surface-current distribution of a target directly from a single optical image. The framework consists of three subnetworks for surface-normal estimation, depth reconstruction, and current refinement, together with an intermediate physics-guided module for coarse current initialization based on physical optics (PO). By progressively embedding geometric cues and electromagnetic priors, the proposed method effectively alleviates the ill-posed geometry-to-current mapping and enforces physical consistency. Extensive numerical experiments demonstrate that the proposed model accurately reconstructs high-fidelity surface currents and radar cross sections (RCS) at 1GHz, achieving agreement comparable to the method of moments (MoM) results while requiring only a single image as input. Experimental validations using 3D-printed objects further confirm the robustness of the framework under real-world measurement uncertainties, including illumination variations and sensor noise. The results show that the proposed method eliminates the need for full geometric modeling and offers strong potential for real-time radar signature prediction, EM simulation acceleration, and autonomous perception.

title: Lightweight Pause Detector-Based ECG Sensing Device for Intelligent Cardiac Health Monitoring
authors: Achinta Mondal, M. Sabarimalai Manikandan, Ram Bilas Pachori
publish: IEEE Sensors Letters
abstract: Sick sinus syndrome and heart blocks are types of heart rhythm disorders (arrhythmia) that are characterized by anirregular rhythm associated with pauses in the electrocardiogram (ECG) signal. The pauses in the ECG signal indicate a delay or complete interruption of electrical impulses from the atria to the ventricles, associated with life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias, with the severity and specific ECG patterns. Therefore, in this letter, we propose a lightweight automatic pause detector-based ECG sensing device by considering the resource constraints of affordable wearable cardiac health monitoring devices. The proposed pause detector consists of preprocessing, bipolar sequence addition, zero-crossing rate, and a decision rule that is integrated with a resource-constrained ECG computing platform. The proposed system achieved a sensitivity of 100% and a specificity of 99.75% across a wide variety of ECG morphologies, including local interval segments and beat-to-beat intervals. Further, evaluation results demonstrated that the pause detector can reduce the false R-peak detection rate by 86.06%-96.19%, leading to reliable R-peak detection without a search-back algorithm. On the Arduino Due (ARM Cortex-M3 CPU, 84 MHz clock, 96 kB SRAM, 512 kB flash) platform, interfacing with the ECG sensor, Bluetooth, and a Smartphone, it consumes energy of 58.85 mJ with a latency of 121 ms for processing a 9 s ECG signal. The proposed pause detector can be integrated with a resource-constrained ECG sensing device to detect the sinus node dysfunctions and heart blocks, and also improves the accuracy and reliability of R-peak detectors.

title: Autonomous Security Framework for AI-Native 6G Networks: Architecture and Threat Taxonomy
authors: Faten Hasni, Arij Salha Nefzi, Akram Hakiri, Abderrazak Jemai
publish: 2026 6th Middle East and North Africa Communications Conference (MENACOMM)
abstract: Sixth-Generation (6 G) networks are envisioned as AI-native systems, where large language models (LLMs) and generative AI orchestrate intent-based networking, autonomous service management, and dynamic slicing. While these capabilities promise unprecedented efficiency and flexibility, they also introduce novel security challenges, i.e., LLM-driven controllers are vulnerable to intent injection, model inversion, and adversarial manipulation, potentially impacting core, edge, and userplane functions. This paper provides a comprehensive review of the security landscape for LLM-driven 6 G networks, mapping known LLM vulnerabilities to telecom network functions, and analyzing approaches for autonomous detection, mitigation, and self-healing. We examine multi-agent red teaming, zero-touch security frameworks, and AI-powered orchestration (SMO), and discuss alignment with O-RAN and ETSI ZSM (Zero-Touch Service Management) standards. Our review identifies open challenges, research trends, and practical considerations for building resilient, self-defending 6 G networks.

title: Numerical Analysis and Performance Optimization of $\text{RbGeBr}_{3}$-Based Perovskite Solar Cells Under Non-Ideal Conditions
authors: Tapos Chandra Saha, Md. Abul Bashar, Ashikur Rahaman Siam, Sadikatul Islam Sara, Md Shahidul Islam, Md. Shafayet Hossen, Sk Minhajul Abedin, Arifuzzaman Rajib
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have become the cell of the future in terms of photovoltaic because of their adjustable bandgap, good optoelectronic characteristics, and low cost of manufacture. <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{RbGeBr}_{3}$</tex> perovskite solar cell was numerically studied through SCAPS-1D simulation software in the present work to determine the influence of the non-ideal conditions and optimize the performance of the device. The suggested design is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F T O} / \mathbf{Z n S e} / \mathbf{R b G e B r}_{\mathbf{3}} / \mathbf{C u}_{\mathbf{2}} \mathbf{O} / \mathbf{N i}$</tex>. Systematic analysis was done to understand the effect of different non-ideal parameters such as optical reflectance, series shunt resistance, bulk defect density, and interface recombination. The impact of the absorber layer which is doped with density and thickness variation. Firstly, analyzed to determine the best absorber characteristics through different ETLs and HTLs layers. Then shows the effect of ZnSe as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{E T L}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{C u}_{2} \mathbf{O}$</tex> as a HTL layer. The analysis of temperature dependence of the device performance has been conducted between temperatures of 278 K and 338 K, and the results indicate a decrease in Voc and PCE with temperature. Lastly, there was a performance comparison of a non-ideal and ideal scenario. The power conversion efficiency (PCE) was increased by 16.25 % in smaller non-ideal situations to 26.94 % with increasing Open-circuit voltage (Voc) 1.20 V and Fill Factor (FF) 85.93% in ideal situations, primarily caused by elimination of reflectance, series-shunt resistance and interface defects. The findings prove that the performance of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{RbGeBr}_{3}$</tex>-based PSCs can be substantially increased with the help of careful interface and defect engineering.

title: SplatFusion: Training-Free 3D Scene Completion From Sparse Views Using Temporal Diffusion Priors and Gaussian Splatting
authors: Tanveer Younis, Dawar Khan, Zhanglin Cheng
publish: 2026 International Conference on 3D Vision (3DV)
abstract: Reconstructing complete 3D scenes from extremely sparse viewpoints (e.g., 2-3 wide-baseline images) remains a core yet unsolved challenge. Existing 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) and neural rendering methods degrade severely when view overlap is limited, often producing incomplete or geometrically distorted results. We introduce SplatFusion, a reconstruction framework that requires no training or fine-tuning of diffusion models, instead relying solely on pretrained video diffusion priors to synthesize missing scene content plausibly. Our core idea is a Scene-Consistent Temporal Guidance (SCTG) mechanism that tightly couples 3D structure with generative diffusion models. Specifically, SCTG conditions video diffusion on sequences rendered from the evolving 3DGS representation, enforcing both spatial alignment with geometry and temporal coherence across synthesized frames. These refined views are back-projected to densify and correct the 3D scene iteratively. Extensive experiments on diverse realworld datasets demonstrate that SplatFusion consistently outperforms existing sparse-view reconstruction methods. Evaluations using VLM-based perceptual scores and the MEt3R metric for geometric consistency show clear gains in visual fidelity and temporal coherence, even in scenarios where previous approaches fail. Our training-free framework opens new possibilities for practical 3D reconstruction applications where dense view acquisition is impractical.

title: Generalized Direct Grid Current Control for Voltage Sensorless Grid-connected Inverter with Output LCL Filter
authors: Jinli Zhu, Bokang Zhou, Yuan Li, Fang Z. Peng, Brad Lehman
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: This paper presents a novel grid voltage sensorless strategy that enables the rapid synchronization and robust control of an LCL-filtered grid-connected inverter. By exclusively measuring the grid current in combination with internal modulation voltage feedforward, the need for AC voltage sensing and PLLs is eliminated, and the inverter control performance under weak grid conditions is significantly improved. The corresponding real-time implementation involves zero-crossing phase detection and fundamental voltage feedforward generation, where the feedforward decoupling effectively enhances the control stability. The performance of the proposed strategy is compared with that of a classical PI control scheme requiring both grid voltage and current sensors. The low-voltage ride-through capability and the robustness to grid impedance variations are verified through simulation. Experimental results effectively validate the performance and reliability of the proposed strategy under normal operation, weak grid, and unbalanced grid conditions.

title: R BD: A Reconstruction-Based Method for Generalizable and Efficient Detection of Fake Images_supp1-3692527.pdf
authors: Zhongjie Ba
publish: N/A
abstract: Recently, reconstruction-based methods have gained attention for AIGC image detection. These methods leverage pre-trained diffusion models to reconstruct inputs and measure residuals for distinguishing real from fake images. Their key advantage lies in reducing reliance on dataset-specific artifacts and improving generalization under distribution shifts. However, they are limited by significant inefficiency due to multi-step inversion and reconstruction, and their reliance on diffusion backbones further limits generalization to other generative paradigms such as GANs. In this paper, we propose a novel fake image detection framework, called R <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> BD, built upon two key designs: (1) G-LDM, a unified reconstruction model that simulates the generation behaviors of VAEs, GANs, and diffusion models, thereby broadening the detection scope beyond prior diffusion-only approaches; and (2) a residual bias calculation module that distinguishes real and fake images in a single inference step, which is a significant efficiency improvement over existing methods that typically require 20<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$+$</tex-math></inline-formula> steps. Extensive experiments on the benchmark from 10 public datasets demonstrate that R <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> BD is over 22× faster than existing reconstruction-based methods while achieving superior detection accuracy. In cross-dataset evaluations, it outperforms state-of-the-art methods by an average of 13.87%, showing strong efficiency and generalization across diverse generative methods. The code and dataset used for evaluation are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/QingyuLiu/RRBD</uri>.

title: Improving Multi-View Reconstruction via Texture-Guided Gaussian-Mesh Joint Optimization
authors: Zhejia Cai, Puhua Jiang, Shiwei Mao, Hongkun Cao, Ruqi Huang
publish: 2026 International Conference on 3D Vision (3DV)
abstract: Reconstructing real-world objects from multi-view images is essential for applications in 3D editing, AR/VR, and digital content creation. Existing methods typically prioritize either geometric accuracy (Multi-View Stereo) or photorealistic rendering (Novel View Synthesis), often decoupling geometry and appearance optimization, which hinders downstream editing tasks. This paper advocates an unified treatment on geometry and appearance optimization for seamless Gaussian-mesh joint optimization. More specifically, we propose a novel framework that simultaneously optimizes mesh geometry (vertex positions and faces) and vertex colors via Gaussian-guided mesh differentiable rendering, leveraging photometric consistency from input images and geometric regularization from normal and depth maps. The obtained high-quality 3D reconstruction can be further exploit in down-stream editing tasks, such as relighting and shape deformation. Our code will be released in https://github.com/zhejia01/TexGuided-GS2Mesh

title: Lightweight Multi-Channel Generative Adversarial Network for Synthesizing Brain Perfusion CT Series from Healthy Subjects: Toward Radiation Dose Reduction in Clinical Imaging_supp1-3696320.docx
authors: Yuanyuan Yang
publish: N/A

title: Structure from Noise: Confirmation Bias in Particle Picking in Structural Biology
authors: Amnon Balanov, Alon Zabatani, Tamir Bendory
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Imaging
abstract: The computational pipelines of single-particle cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) and cryo-electron tomography (cryo-ET) include an early particle-picking stage, in which a micrograph or tomogram is scanned to extract candidate particles, typically via template matching or deep-learning-based techniques. The extracted particles are then passed to downstream tasks such as classification and 3D reconstruction. Although it is well understood empirically that particle picking can be sensitive to the choice of templates or learned priors, a quantitative theory of the bias introduced by this stage has been lacking. Here, we develop a mathematical framework for analyzing bias in template matching-based detection with concrete applications to cryo-EM and cryo-ET. We study this bias through two downstream tasks: (i) maximum-likelihood estimation of class means in a Gaussian mixture model and (ii) 3D volume reconstruction from the extracted particle stack. We show that when template matching is applied to pure noise, then under broad noise models, the resulting maximum-likelihood estimates converge asymptotically to deterministic, noise-dependent transforms of the user-specified templates, yielding a structure from noise effect. We further characterize how the resulting bias depends on the noise statistics, sample size, dimension, and detection threshold. Finally, controlled experiments using standard cryo-EM software corroborate the theory, demonstrating reproducible structure from noise artifacts in low-SNR data.

title: T-LEAP Posit: A Truncated Logarithmic Enhanced Arithmetic Posit Multiplier for Area–Power Efficient and Accuracy-Robust Deep Neural Networks.
authors: Thalla Narasimha Swetha, Vignav Ramkumar, Syed Ershad Ahmed
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Posit arithmetic offers improved numerical accuracy and dynamic range compared to IEEE 754 floating-point representation. However, hardware implementation of posit multiplication remains complex due to costly decoding and mantissa computation stages, limiting its suitability for resource-constrained systems. This limitation becomes critical in applications such as deep learning inference,where low delay and reduced chip area are often more critical than very high arithmetic precision. In many deep neural networks, increasing multiplier precision beyond a certain level does not lead to maintaining the model accuracy. As a result, implementing fully precise arithmetic circuits can increase hardware cost without providing improvement in inference accuracy. To address this challenge, an efficient approximate posit multiplier is designed to reduce hardware overhead while preserving computational accuracy for deep neural networks. The proposed architecture integrates a posit decoder that replaces conventional regime extraction by eliminating adder, multiplexer and complement operation, with a compact XOR-driven approach with leading-bit removal of regime bits, thereby reducing hardware overhead and improving energy efficiency. A new logarithmic-truncated mantissa multiplier that uses truncated shifter-based operations for logarithmic multiplication is proposed, significantly reducing hardware complexity. In the input decoding stage, a streamlined XOR-based approach achieves a reduction in power–delay product of 50-61% and area reduction of 3-20% compared to existing decoders. The proposed T-LEAP multiplier demonstrates a notable reduction in hardware cost, achieving up to 20-46% lower area, 21-66% lower power consumption, 11-19% delay reduction, and 31-73% improvement in power-delay product (PDP) compared to existing posit multiplier architectures. Experimental results show that the deep neural networks using T-LEAP multiplier maintain over 90% classification accuracy on LeNet-5 and ResNet-20 across different benchmark datasets such as MNIST and CIFAR-10. These findings indicate that T-LEAP multiplier offers an effective trade-off between hardware efficiency and computational accuracy, making it a promising solution for deploying deep neural networks.

title: Satellite-Derived Chlorophyll-a and Deep Learning for Enhanced Potential Fishing Zone Advisory in the Bay of Bengal
authors: Rafiul Awal, Nabila Rahman, Md. Maruf Rusafi Arnob, Md Abrar Nawar, Protiva Roy, Hafsa Binte Rashid
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Bay of Bengal resources support Bangladesh marine fisheries, but Potential Fishing Zones (PFZs) identification remains difficult because of oceanographic variability at a seasonal scale. Existing threshold-based advisories created using sea surface temperature and chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) concentration are ineffective for spatio-temporal dynamics, particularly at monsoon transitions. In this work, we employ MODIS-Aqua Chl-a data (July–December 2023) and develop deep learning models—U-Net, ConvLSTM, DeepLabv3+, HRNetv2-w18, and SigFormer—to identify PFZ. The proposed framework allows seasonal PFZ pattern mapping, distinguishes monsoon–post-monsoon contrast, and provides high-resolution hotspot maps. Experimental results show that deep learning techniques are more accurate than traditional methods in boundary detection and spatial acuteness and demonstrate temporal generalization by forecasting PFZs in December from models learned using July–November data. The present work provides a foundation for developing dynamic PFZ advisory systems that can support sustainable marine fisheries in Bangladesh.

title: NeuralFur: Animal Fur Reconstruction from Multi-View Images
authors: Vanessa Sklyarova, Berna Kabadayi, Anastasios Yiannakidis, Giorgio Becherini, Michael J. Black, Justus Thies
publish: 2026 International Conference on 3D Vision (3DV)
abstract: Reconstructing realistic animal fur geometry from images is a challenging task due to the fine-scale details, selfocclusion, and view-dependent appearance of fur. In contrast to human hairstyle reconstruction, there are also no datasets that can be leveraged to learn a fur prior for different animals. In this work, we present a first multi-viewbased method for high-fidelity 3D fur modeling of animals using a strand-based representation, leveraging the general knowledge of a vision language model. Given multiview RGB images, we first reconstruct a coarse surface geometry using traditional multi-view stereo techniques. We then use a vision language model (VLM) system to retrieve information about the realistic length structure of the fur for each part of the body. We use this knowledge to construct the animal's furless geometry and grow strands atop it. The fur reconstruction is supervised with both geometric and photometric losses computed from multi-view images. To mitigate orientation ambiguities stemming from the Gabor filters that are applied to the input images, we additionally utilize the VLM to guide the strands' growth direction and their relation to the gravity vector that we incorporate as a loss. With this new schema of using a VLM to guide 3D reconstruction from multi-view inputs, we show generalization across a variety of animals with different fur types. For additional results and code, please refer to https://neuralfur.is.tue.mpg.de.

title: Stable Learning for Trustworthy Modeling in Industrial Processes With Small Samples
authors: Liang Cao, Youqing Wang, Yankai Cao, Fan Yang, Jicong Fan, Yan Qin, Bhushan Gopaluni, Richard D. Braatz
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: Developing trustworthy models for industrial processes is challenging due to changing operating conditions and limited data. In this work, we present an innovative and effective framework for establishing trustworthy process models from limited data. Our approach introduces the uniform manifold approximation and projection (UMAP) algorithm to uncover the essential low-dimensional structure in real industrial data, which allows high-quality virtual sample generation (VSG) that captures the underlying process dynamics. Furthermore, we propose a new algorithm for stable learning that uses sample reweighting to effectively mitigate spurious correlations that can undermine the stability and reliability of the model. Two case studies, the Tennessee Eastman process (TEP) and a commercial fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) unit, demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework. In the Tennessee Eastman benchmark, the proposed method reduces the average error by 12.5% compared with VSG alone and by 47.8% compared with the OLS baseline. In the FCC case study, it further achieves a 41.5% RMSE reduction compared with the strongest competing UMAP-augmented model. Furthermore, the proposed framework maintains robust performance under changing operating conditions. Our framework enables the development of trustworthy industrial process models from limited data, which offers a powerful method to improve operational safety and efficiency in real-world applications.

title: Anchor-Driven Compact Gaussian Splatting for Dynamic Scene Reconstruction_supp1-3695021.mp4
authors: Yiling Xu
publish: N/A
abstract: Existing 4D Gaussian Splatting methods typically rely on per-Gaussian deformation from a canonical space to target frames, which overlooks the strong redundancy among spatially and temporally adjacent Gaussian primitives and leads to suboptimal efficiency. To address this limitation, we propose ADC-GS++, an anchor-driven compact Gaussian splatting frame work for efficient and high-quality dynamic scene reconstruction. Specifically, ADC-GS++ organizes Gaussian primitives into an anchor-based canonical representation, enabling attribute sharing across local regions. To efficiently model dynamic scenes, we introduce a static–dynamic decomposition mechanism and further employ a coarse-to-fine deformation strategy driven by dynamic anchors at multiple granularities. In addition, a unified rate-distortion optimization is adopted to achieve a balanced trade-off between storage efficiency and reconstruction fidelity. Furthermore, a temporal significance-based anchor refinement strategy is employed to dynamically grow and prune anchors, allowing robust adaptation to complex and large-scale motions. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world dynamic scene datasets demonstrate that ADC-GS++ significantly improves rendering speed over deformation-based approaches by 300% 700%, while maintaining competitive rendering quality. More over, ADC-GS++ achieves a more favorable rate-distortion trade off, resulting in substantially reduced storage consumption across different bitrate settings.

title: Toward Explainable Trust: Polynomial-Driven Bidomain Memory-Gain-Scheduling of Active Suspension Systems via Mode-Affected Physical-Layer Authentication
authors: Xingchen Shao, Xiang-Peng Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: The evolution of 6G networks empowers the integration of sensing, communication, computing, and control, driving mining vehicles toward unprecedented mobility. Although this deep wireless integration enhances operational efficiency, it comes at the cost of breaking down the physical isolation of vehicular control loops. Such architectural openness invalidates the conventional trust-by-default assumption, directly exposing active suspension systems to mode-affected cyber-physical threats that secretly manipulate operational data. To reconstruct systemic reliability and shift the overall paradigm toward explainable trust, this article proposes a resilience-oriented co-design scheme. Guided by the principle of building trusted sensing through authentication, we establish a mode-affected signature of hardware emanation (MA-SHE) model. This model quantitatively characterizes the residual risk, explicitly explaining the necessary tradeoff between security stringency and real-time control availability. To execute the concept of using control mechanisms to mitigate authentication inaccuracies, we synthesize a polynomial-driven memory-gain-scheduling strategy. This strategy dynamically compensates for the quantified vulnerabilities, guaranteeing suspension stability even when the authentication tolerates a subset of spoofed signals. Furthermore, to handle the complex transition probabilities inherent in this stochastic environment, we develop a bidomain framework. It reconstructs the incomplete probability matrix via polytopic formulation in the structural domain and utilizes homogeneous-polynomial theory in the design domain, substantially enlarging the feasible solution space. Hardware-in-the-loop experiments validate that the proposed framework drastically reduces the conservative attenuation index and improves vibration suppression by approximately 20%, embedding explainable trust into the closed-loop architecture to ensure dynamic resilience.

title: Long-Reach, High-Capacity Non-Orthogonal PAM4–OFDM Optical Access Transmission Enabled by Advanced Chromatic Dispersion Pre-compensation
authors: Joungmoon Lee, Jinwoo Park, Sang-Kook Han
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: We propose an advanced non-orthogonal transmission framework for converged wired (four-level pulse amplitude modulation, PAM4) and wireless (radio-over-fiber orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing) services in long-reach passive optical networks. In contrast to conventional approaches that focus primarily on signal restoration, the proposed method re-engineers chromatic dispersion pre-compensation to suppress mutual interference, thereby enlarging the interference-free overlapping bandwidth available to heterogeneous traffic. By leveraging the distance-dependent power-fading nulls in C-band optical transmission, spectral coexistence is achieved without conventional orthogonal resource allocation. Experimental validation over a 70-km single-mode fiber link demonstrates that both services maintain bit error rates below their respective forward error correction (FEC) thresholds despite stringent hardware constraints. Specifically, the 12.5-Gbaud PAM4 signal exhibits a negligible power penalty of less than 1 dB at the soft-decision FEC threshold relative to the optical back-to-back reference. These results confirm that the proposed scheme provides a robust solution for high-capacity converged optical access networks.

title: CoDA: Color Distribution Probing for Efficient and Generalizable AI-Generated Image Detection
authors: Zexi Jia, Zhiqiang Yuan, Xiaoyue Duan, Jinchao Zhang, Jie Zhou, Anil K. Jain
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: AI-generated image detection faces a persistent trade-off between generalization and efficiency: lightweight artifact-based methods often degrade on unseen generators or domains, whereas more robust large-scale models are computationally expensive. Meanwhile, existing benchmarks mainly focus on cross-model evaluation in photorealistic settings, leaving cross-domain robustness underexplored. To address this gap, we introduce FakeForm, a large-scale benchmark with approximately 370,000 images across 62 diverse domains for both cross-model and cross-domain evaluation. Motivated by this broader setting, we revisit color-distribution probing as an efficient complementary cue for AI-generated image detection. We observe that, especially for photographic content, real photographs tend to exhibit smoother and more stable color patterns, whereas synthetic images often show characteristic color imbalances introduced by neural generation. Based on this observation, we propose CoDA, a compact 1.48M-parameter detector built on a Noise-Quantization Probe, together with a theoretical analysis linking probe responses to color non-uniformity. Experiments show that CoDA achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard benchmarks and the best results on the challenging cross-domain evaluation of FakeForm, while remaining highly competitive in cross-model photorealistic settings. These results suggest that persistent generative artifacts can provide a practical foundation for efficient and robust AI-generated image detection. The models and FakeForm benchmark will be made publicly available.

title: Strong electric field measurement method based on Rydberg atom Stark effect
authors: Sheng Yan, Dongping Xiao, Huaiqing Zhang, Dongyang Wen
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Quantum electric field measurement based on the Stark effect of Rydberg atoms has the advantages of high precision, traceability, and self-calibration. However, the current perturbation-based modeling method for the relationship between field strength and energy level shift only applies to weak fields. In applications such as power systems, electric fields can reach the kV/m level. Under such electric fields, the Rydberg state cannot ignore the strong coupling effect between adjacent high l states, and the relationship model established by perturbation approximation is no longer accurate. This paper introduces the Landau–Zener (L-Z) tunneling model to address this limitation and describes the coupling between energy levels. It is used to construct the Rydberg atomic Hamiltonian, which includes high l state coupling. We calculate the eigenenergies of Rydberg atoms at various electric-field strengths via matrix diagonalization, thereby obtaining a discrete mapping between field strength and energy-level shift in the strong-field regime. Subsequently, the hybrid Levenberg–Marquardt (LM) and Bayesian polynomial regression (BPR) neural network (LM-BPR) was constructed to train the discrete mapping into a continuous prediction model. In addition, the dynamic programming (DP) algorithm performs global ridge tracking on the electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT) spectrum and extracts frequency shift information from the complex EIT spectrum. Combining the above methods, strong electric field inversion based on Rydberg atoms was achieved. Experiments using the 28D5/2 Rydberg state demonstrate electric-field measurements up to 7 kV/m, with a relative error of 2.9%.

title: Attention-Based CNN Architectures for Automated Binary Classification of Salmon Fish Diseases
authors: Jasmin Jahan Puspo, Diya Rahman
publish: 2025 IEEE International Women in Engineering (WIE) Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering (WIECON-ECE)
abstract: Fish health is important in aquaculture, and early disease detection can mitigate large-scale losses. In this study, an image dataset, SalmonScan, is utilized, which is divided into two classes: fresh salmon and infected salmon. A novel model has been proposed based on CNN architectures (InceptionV3, ResNet50, DenseNet-121) with convolutional block attention module (CBAM) mechanism. Experimental results show that InceptionV3 provides a superior classification accuracy of 96.50 % on training, and 92.40 % on testing. These findings demonstrate that attention-based deep learning methods can provide an effective tool for fish health diagnosis, potentially assist farmers make timely decisions, and improve aquaculture productivity.

title: Long-Term Optimal Incentives for Differential-Privacy Federated Learning: A Multi-Stage Game Approach
authors: Liang Xie, Yuntao Wang, Hengzhi Wang, Laizhong Cui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: Differential-privacy federated learning (DP-FL) has emerged as a promising paradigm capable of mitigating the inherent threat of traditional FL architectures that are vulnerable to inferential attacks due to the frequent exchange and updating of model parameters. However, existing DP-FL frameworks often assume that the client's perturbations remain constant throughout the FL process, while ignoring the varying influence of the client's perturbations in distinct communication rounds on the model performance. Besides, existing DP-FL frameworks posit the FL server as a fully rational actor, thereby neglecting the bounded rationality that the FL server may exhibit in the face of risk and uncertainty. In this paper, we propose a novel long-term (i.e., throughout the FL process) privacy-preserving FL framework to address the optimal incentive design, in the presence of the bounded rationality inherent in the FL server and the dynamic influence of perturbations on model performance. Specifically, we first investigate the impact of local perturbations of the client on the model's convergence performance in different communication rounds, elucidating the trade-off between learning performance and privacy loss. Then, to reconcile learning performance with privacy loss, we design a long-term privacy-preserving incentive scheme, where the interactions between clients and the FL server throughout the FL process are modeled as a multi-stage privacy-preserving game. Furthermore, by applying prospect theory (PT) to formulate the risk-aware behavior of the bounded rationality FL server, we employ contract theory to derive the equilibrium of the game, thereby ensuring optimality and fairness. Finally, extensive simulations illustrate that our scheme can motivate clients to provide high-quality models and improve the accuracy of the global model, compared with benchmarks.

title: MEMS-Based Gas Sensing via Broadband Dielectric Relaxation Spectroscopy
authors: Satish K. Verma, Mahesh Menon, Aravind N. Warrier, Andreas Mrosk, Markus Gardill, Harald Schenk
publish: IEEE Sensors Letters
abstract: Reliable and selective gas sensing demands a low-cost, energy-efficient, single-unit multi-gas sensor robust to ambient condition variations. This paper presents a novel broadband dielectric spectroscopy-based gas sensing platform. The sensor leverages strong localized electric fields (> 106 V/m) across microscale gaps under a hybrid bias (0.5 V ac + 5 V dc), inducing polarization within the gas-domain at ambient temperature. It employs MEMS based parallel comb electrodes for the detection and characterization of gas-phase analytes, demonstrated here with acetone vapor. An acetone specific loss peak at ∼350 Hz, attributed to gas-phase dielectric relaxation, scales with acetone concentration with sensitivity of 33.7m/ppm. This mechanism is promising for a miniaturized, low power, label-free, frequency-resolved dielectric sensing framework, scalable for multi-analyte, CMOS-integrated, chip-scale sensor systems.

title: Optical Remote Sensing Image Super-Resolution via Adaptive High-Pass Filtering and Window Attention Mechanism
authors: Qikai Zhou, Baoxiang Huang, He Gao, Milena Radenkovic, Ge Chen
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Remote sensing image super-resolution (SR) aims to enhance image resolution, critical for various applications. Conventional convolutional neural networks (CNNs) often struggle to recover fine textures and sharp edges, and are limited in adaptively combining local and global features. To address this, we propose Adaptive High-Pass Spatial Gate Transformer Network (AHGTNet), a novel cascade network for optical remote sensing image SR. AHGTNet integrates an Adaptive High-Pass Enhancement Block (AHEB) and a Residual Gate Window Attention Blocks (RGWB). The AHEB dynamically extracts and emphasizes high-frequency features, crucial for detailed textures and edges, by generating adaptive filtering weights and applying grouped high-pass operations. Subsequently, the RGWB efficiently models both local details and long-range dependencies, facilitating a comprehensive integration of contextual features. Extensive experiments on three optical remote sensing datasets demonstrate AHGTNet's effectiveness. Compared with state-of-the-art methods, our model consistently achieves competitive, and often leading, reconstruction quality in terms of PSNR and SSIM. Furthermore, AHGTNet shows favorable performance on real-world SR tasks, adeptly recovering detailed textures while preserving overall scene coherence.

title: Broadband Microwave Signal Processing via Low-Frequency-Detected Photonic Correlation in Parallel Bidirectional Frequency-Shifting Loops
authors: Di Peng, Zewen Zhu, Jingyi Zhang, Shuoyang Qiu, Lingjie Zhang, Songnian Fu, Yuwen Qin
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: We propose and demonstrate a photonic correlator based on parallel optical bidirectional frequency-shifting loops (BFSLs) and low-frequency detection, which enables broadband microwave signal processing with an extended time window. By mapping correlation results to low-frequency spectral envelopes, the system avoids the need for high-speed electronics. Precise control of relative delays in each BFSL ensures high temporal resolution and broad operating bandwidth. Moreover, the overall time window is significantly expanded via sequentially stitching spectra from multiple BFSLs. Both numerical simulation and experimental verification based on two parallel BFSLs confirm the feasibility of the scheme. Experiments demonstrate autocorrelation of sinusoidal signals, band-limited white noise, linear frequency-modulated (LFM) signals, and fourth-order raised-cosine shaped nonlinear frequency-modulated signals, achieving a doubled time window with temporal resolution ranging from 20 ps to 1 ns. In a radar ranging scenario using LFM signals centered at 4-10 GHz and with 1.5-4 GHz bandwidth, the system achieves a ranging error of 0.88 cm, a range resolution of 0.375 cm, and a time-window extension factor of 2. The scheme offers a practical solution for high-resolution broadband correlation with an extended time window, showing great potential for advanced radar and communication systems.

title: Innovation-Domain Decision-Directed Phase Tracking for Wiener Phase Noise in Fast Rayleigh Fading
authors: Ura Klongklaew, Nithiroth Pornsuwancharoen, Phichai Youplao
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: This letter proposes an innovation-domain decision-directed phase tracking (ID-DDPT) architecture for coherent detection over Rayleigh fading channels with temporally correlated phase evolution and Wiener phase noise. By reformulating phase tracking into the innovation domain, replacing the unbounded cumulative phase with its stationary increments, the proposed method converts a non-stationary estimation problem into a stable low-complexity filtering problem. A closed-form expression for the steady-state residual phase error variance is derived under the locked-regime assumption, along with an analytical optimal smoothing factor. Modeling the residual phase distortion as an effective signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) attenuation yields a tractable bit error rate (BER) approximation for BPSK over Rayleigh fading. A first-order error-propagation analysis further characterizes the impact of decision errors and provides insight into the onset of cycle slips. Simulation results demonstrate that ID-DDPT outperforms DBPSK and a complexity-equivalent scalar Kalman tracker (SKT), achieving near-coherent performance with O(1) per-symbol complexity and minimal pilot overhead.

title: Full Index Mapping Multi-Domain AFDM-IM for High-Mobility Communications
authors: Shunyan Huang, Wenjing Zheng, Taojie Zhu, Ting Qi, Lei Wang
publish: IEEE Communications Letters
abstract: Affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM) is an emerging multicarrier modulation technique for high-mobility communications. However, existing AFDM index modulation (IM) schemes suffer from two critical limitations: unreliable index detection caused by subcarrier activation and limited index-space expansion due to single-domain indexing. To overcome both issues simultaneously, this letter proposes an enhanced multi-domain (EMD) IM scheme for AFDM, termed EMD-AFDM-IM. Unlike existing AFDM-IM schemes, EMD-AFDM-IM adopts fully active subcarriers and conveys additional index bits through a novel joint multi-domain indexing mechanism. Furthermore, a dedicated low-complexity progressive hybrid detection algorithm (PHDA) is developed, and the average bit error probability (ABEP) of the proposed scheme is analyzed. Simulation results validate the analysis and demonstrate that EMD-AFDM-IM significantly outperforms state-of-the-art benchmark schemes over doubly dispersive channels, achieving notable signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) gains.

title: Beamforming Algorithm and System Optimization Based on True-Time-Delay and Phase Cooperative Control for Broadband Optically Controlled Phased Arrays
authors: Ting An, Limin Liu, Yunfeng Jiang, Yunjuan Zhang
publish: IEEE Photonics Journal
abstract: To address the contradiction between hardware non-idealities and broadband beam squint effects in microwave photonic beamforming systems, this paper proposes a beamforming algorithm framework based on true-time-delay and phase cooperative control. In this paper, we decouple and analyze the discrete tuning characteristics of optical true-time delay lines and the continuous phase tuning capability of electro-optic phase modulators, a mapping model from device constraints to system performance is established. A three-layer algorithm architecture is proposed, including discrete true-time delay solving, improved delay-and-sum algorithm, and robust MVDR algorithm. On this basis, a true-time-delay and phase cooperative control strategy is constructed, utilizing true-time delay to eliminate beam squint and electro-optic phase modulator to compensate for time-delay quantization residual errors. Simulation results show that the cooperative control reduces the beam pointing error from 0.86° to 0.06°, suppresses the sidelobe level to −31.5 dB, and achieves a squint coefficient lower than 0.01 ° /GHz. This study provides theoretical and algorithmic support for high-precision, low sidelobe, wide-angle scanning of broadband optically controlled phased array systems.

title: Flexbee: A Grasping and Perching UAV Based on Soft Vector-Propulsion Nozzle
authors: Yue Wang, Lixian Zhang, Yangguang Liu, Xuwei Yang, Yimin Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: To improve environmental adaptability maneuverability, and structural reuse, for cooperative tasks in existing grasping/perching uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs), we propose a soft vector-propulsion nozzle (SVPN) and develop Flexbee, a grasping and perching UAV built upon this concept, and it enables fully-actuated flight as well as grasping and perching. By redesigning both the mechanism and the system architecture, the SVPN consolidates multiple functions into a single modular unit, thereby expanding the achievable grasping range and improving perching versatility compared with add-on, functionally independent mechanisms. Moreover, fully-actuated flight enhances controllability and operational robustness during aerial interaction. This study covers the complete system design, including mechanical design, dynamic modeling, and controller design, which demonstrates the novelty and feasibility of the proposed platform through real-world experiments.

title: The Flashover characteristics and arc development patterns of train roof insulator under ultra - high altitude
authors: Yujun Guo, Ningning Su, Limei Hong, Ye Cao, Chuan Zhang, Xueqin Zhang, Guoqiang Gao, Guangning Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: With the continuous expansion of railway networks, trains are increasingly operating in extreme environments exceeding 4000 m in altitude, where roof insulators face the challenge of significantly reduced air pressure. Therefore, the flashover characteristics and arc propagation behavior of roof insulators are investigated under ultra - high altitude conditions based on tests in an artificial climate chamber. First, flashover tests were performed on four types of roof insulators with different shed structures under varying air pressure and contamination levels. Subsequently, the effects of different shed structures on flashover voltage and arc development under low - pressure conditions were analyzed. Last, the effective utilization rate of creepage distance, denoted as kE, is proposed to quantify the influence of the arc - floatation effect in ultra - high altitude environments. It is indicated that the flashover voltage of roof insulators decreases more significantly under ultra - high altitude conditions, as air pressure characteristics impact index n exhibits an initial increase followed by a decrease when the equivalent salt deposit density (ESDD) increases. The effective utilization rate of creepage distance kE decreases following a power-law trend with reducing pressure. If the creepage distance is sufficient, appropriately increasing the shed spacing and large shed diameter can mitigate the impact of the arc - floatation effect, improving insulation performance in ultra - high altitude environments. Conclusion above can provide valuable references for the design and performance evaluation of roof insulators intended for use in such demanding conditions.

title: Robust Fine-Grained Oriented Ship Detection for Remote Sensing imagery via Controllable Generative Pretraining_supp1-3694663.docx
authors: Xiaoping Liu
publish: N/A
abstract: Fine-grained ship recognition in remote sensing imagery is essential for maritime applications. However, its development is hindered by two challenges: (1) the limited granularity of existing ship detection datasets, and (2) the disturbance of complex maritime conditions as well as the arbitrary ship orientations and distributions. To address the first issue, we annotated a large-scale fine-grained ship instance detection dataset (LAFI), comprising 48,717 ship instances worldwide with 49 categories. To tackle the challenges of marine disturbance and diverse ship status, we proposed a controllable generative knowledge-driven ship detection framework (COSD). It employs a controllable generative model guided by ship-marine knowledge to generate millions of synthetic images that not only preserve ship structures but also cover diverse sea and weather conditions for robust pretraining. Furthermore, a heterogeneous feature alignment decoder is designed to align multi-modal metrics of orientation and distribution features in the latent space, allowing for accurate representation of diverse ship status. Extensive experiments on two benchmark datasets showed that our method respectively increased 0.093 and 0.129 mean average precision (mAP) over SOTA methods, particularly in scenarios involving small, densely packed and arbitrary oriented ships.

title: Optimizing Power Infrastructure Reinforcement using Distributed Generators: An Economic Loss-Based Analysis of Three Installation Scenarios
authors: Gayathri Bhaskar, Sivasankari GS, Narayanan K, Mithulananthan Nadarajah
publish: 2026 IEEE 6th International Conference in Power Engineering Applications (ICPEA)
abstract: Distributed generators (DGs) have emerged as an effective solution for supporting load growth in modern distribution systems by supplying power close to demand centers. When optimally placed and sized, DGs can reduce power losses, improve voltage profiles and enhance the load hosting capacity of the network. This study investigates the role of DG integration in managing increased demand by evaluating the economic implications of different DG installation strategies. The maximum allowable load increase in the system is determined while considering ampacity and power flow constraints. Based on this analysis, three DG installation scenarios are examined to assess their impact on economic losses associated with infrastructure reinforcement. The results demonstrate that strategic DG deployment can significantly influence the cost-effectiveness of accommodating load growth within existing distribution networks. This study provides valuable insights for distribution planners seeking economically efficient approaches to support future load expansion.

title: Mango Leaf Disease Classification through EfficientNetB5 based Deep Learning Framework
authors: Bhoomika, Sripelli Jagadish, Tashi Negi, Garima Jaitly, Neha Sharma, Sheifali Gupta
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: Mango (Mangifera indica L.) is economically; this denotes that it is one of the most important fruit crops in the tropics and whose yield and quality is extremely influenced by a number of foliar diseases. The early and proper identification of these diseases is the key to the effects since it guarantees healthy crops and economic agricultural activities. This research paper suggests EfficientNetB5 deep learning model that is fine tuned for classification of 3 classes of mango leaves diseases automatically into 8 different categories namely Anthracnose, Bacterial Canker, Cutting Weevil, Die Back, Gall Midge, Healthy, Powdery Mildew & Sooty Mould. The dataset is an open-source of Kaggle with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4, 0 0 0}$</tex> high-quality images, consisting of 500 samples of each class, and organized in training (80 %), validation (10 %), and testing (10 %) groups of images. Transfer learning, data augmentation, and regularization were used to fine-tune the model in order to improve the feature extraction and the generalization. In experiments the great accuracy of 97 % was obtained, the precision, recall and F 1 -score were all 0.97. Minimal misclassifications have been indicated by the confusion matrix that depicts outstanding discriminative precision among a visually similar disease. The convergence of the training and validation plots proved to be constant with minimum loss. Altogether, the suggested EfficientNetB5-based context has a high level of reliability and efficiency and can be seen as an effective instrument to utilize a timely mango leaf disease recognition and encourage a precision agricultural approach and sustainable farming habits.

title: On the Compressibility of Fine-Tuned Attention: A Tensor Decomposition Study of PEFT Weight Structure
authors: Kasun Dewage, Shankhadeep Mondal
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Natural Language Processing (ICNLP)
abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA rest on the premise that adaptation lives in a low-rank subspace. An increasingly popular line of work takes this further, using tensor decompositions such as Tucker and Tensor-Train to parameterise even more compact updates. Yet surprisingly little is known about whether the resulting fine-tuned weights actually possess the low-rank tensor structure these methods presuppose. We investigate this question through a careful empirical study of RoBERTa-Base fine-tuned on eight GLUE tasks. After stacking all attention projections into a single third-order tensor, we examine its spectral profile, geometric properties, and behaviour under decomposition. Three findings stand out. First, the merged attention matrices have high effective rank (above 520 out of 768), with gradually decaying singular value spectra that lack any clear low-rank cutoff. Second, naive post-hoc Tucker and Tensor-Train compressions are devastating: even a gentle 1.5 times reduction can wipe out more than 40 percentage points of accuracy. Third, the weight-change tensor, while sparse across projection types, carries task-specific geometric signatures that PCA and CKA analyses make plainly visible. These observations challenge the casual assumption that attention weights are nearly low-rank and point toward decomposition-aware training as the right path for tensor-based PEFT.

title: Single Event Upset Characterization of the Versal® Adaptive SoC Using Proton Irradiation
authors: David M. Hiemstra, Nelson Hu
publish: 2025 IEEE Radiation Effects Data Workshop (REDW) (in conjunction with 2025 NSREC)
abstract: Proton induced SEU cross-sections of the SRAM which stores the logic configuration and certain functional blocks of the Versal® Adaptive SoC Processing System and Programmable Logic are presented. Upset rate in the space radiation environment is estimated.
